META.subject: Mathematics
META.subtitleDoc: CBE Phenomenon-Driven Lesson Sequence — Sub-Strand 3.1 (10 Lessons)

UNIT.subject: Mathematics
UNIT.content:
• Trigonometric ratios of acute angles (sine, cosine, tangent defined from right-angled triangles)
• Sine of an acute angle — definition, values, and use of mathematical tables
• Cosine of an acute angle — definition, values, and use of mathematical tables
• Tangent of an acute angle — definition, values, and use of mathematical tables
• Finding trigonometric ratios using calculators
• Relationship between sine, cosine and tangent of acute angles (tan θ = sin θ / cos θ)
• Trigonometric ratios of complementary angles (sin θ = cos(90° − θ))
• Special angles (0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°) — exact values without tables
• Angles of elevation and depression
• Application of trigonometry in real-life situations (heights, distances, surveying)
UNIT.learningOutcomes:
By the end of the sub-strand, the learner should be able to:
a) define sine, cosine and tangent of an acute angle using measurements from right-angled triangles drawn by hand,
b) determine trigonometric ratios of acute angles using mathematical tables,
c) determine trigonometric ratios of acute angles using a calculator,
d) establish the relationship between sine, cosine and tangent of acute angles (tan θ = sin θ / cos θ),
e) identify and apply the relationship between trigonometric ratios of complementary angles (sin θ = cos(90° − θ)),
f) derive and recall exact trigonometric ratios for special angles (0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°),
g) distinguish between angles of elevation and angles of depression in diagrams and real contexts,
h) apply trigonometric ratios to solve problems involving heights and distances in real-life Kenyan contexts,
i) appreciate the relevance of trigonometry in architecture, surveying, navigation, and everyday problem-solving.
UNIT.coreCompetencies:
• Communication and Collaboration: learners work in groups to draw right-angled triangles of chosen measurements, measure angles with a protractor, share computed ratios, and discuss patterns observed across different triangles.
• Critical Thinking and Problem Solving: learners analyse why opposite/hypotenuse always gives the same ratio for a fixed angle regardless of triangle size, and use this insight to solve height-and-distance problems set in Kenyan contexts (e.g., estimating the height of a flagpole at a Nairobi school).
• Digital Literacy: learners use scientific calculators and, where available, digital devices to determine trigonometric ratios and verify table values.
• Learning to Learn: learners derive exact values for special angles independently using equilateral and isosceles right-angled triangles, building self-directed inquiry habits.
• Self-efficacy: learners gain confidence in applying abstract angle ratios to concrete, visible real-world situations such as measuring the height of a building or a hill in the Rift Valley.
UNIT.values:
• Responsibility: learners handle protractors, rulers, calculators, and mathematical tables carefully during hands-on triangle-drawing activities, returning equipment in good condition.
• Integrity: learners record actual measured values honestly, even when results differ slightly from expected ratios, and reflect on sources of measurement error.
• Unity: learners collaborate in diverse groups during the triangle-drawing phenomenon activity and the special-angles derivation task, valuing each member's contribution.
• Respect: learners listen to peers' explanations during sensemaking discussions and appreciate different approaches to solving trigonometric problems.
• Social Justice: learners recognise how trigonometry empowers communities — e.g., local surveyors and engineers in Kisumu or Mombasa use these tools — making mathematics relevant beyond the classroom.
UNIT.sep:
• Planning and Carrying Out Investigations: learners design and conduct a hands-on investigation by drawing right-angled triangles of self-chosen dimensions, measuring angles with a protractor, computing ratios of sides, and recording results systematically in a table.
• Analysing and Interpreting Data: learners compare sine, cosine, and tangent values obtained from their own triangles with table/calculator values, identifying patterns (e.g., sin and cos of complementary angles are equal) and resolving discrepancies.
• Using Mathematics and Computational Thinking: learners use ratio, proportion, and algebraic manipulation (tan θ = sin θ / cos θ) to connect the three trigonometric functions, and apply these to calculate unknown heights and distances.
• Constructing Explanations: learners articulate in writing and speech why the ratio of sides remains constant for a given angle regardless of triangle size, grounding their explanation in the concept of similar triangles.
• Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information: learners read articles and watch videos on complementary angles, special angles, and angles of elevation/depression, then synthesise information into annotated diagrams and model sketches.
UNIT.pcis:
• Life Skills (Critical Thinking and Problem Solving): learners apply step-by-step logical reasoning to set up and solve angle-of-elevation and angle-of-depression problems, building real-world decision-making skills.
• Life Skills (Self-Esteem): learners gain confidence as they successfully derive exact special-angle values using geometric reasoning rather than memorisation, reinforcing belief in their own mathematical ability.
• Career Guidance: learners explore careers — surveying, architecture, engineering, aviation, and game-park tourism (e.g., tracking animal positions using bearings at Maasai Mara) — where trigonometry is directly applied.
• Environmental Education: learners consider how trigonometry supports responsible land surveying and forest-boundary mapping in Kenya, linking mathematics to sustainable land use in areas such as the Aberdare Range.
UNIT.careers:
• Land Surveyor: uses trigonometric ratios daily to measure distances and heights across Kenyan terrain — from Rift Valley escarpments to coastal plains near Mombasa.
• Civil/Structural Engineer: applies sine, cosine, and tangent when designing bridges, road gradients, and building foundations for infrastructure projects such as those along the SGR corridor.
• Architect: uses angles of elevation and trigonometric ratios to calculate roof pitches, ramp gradients, and shadow lengths for building designs in Nairobi and other urban centres.
• Aviation Pilot / Air Traffic Controller: uses angles of depression and elevation for approach paths and altitude calculations at airports including JKIA and Kisumu International Airport.
• Geologist / Mining Engineer: applies trigonometry when mapping rock strata angles and calculating depths at mining sites in Taita Hills or the Lake Victoria basin.
• Game-Park Tourism Guide / Wildlife Researcher: uses angular measurements and distance estimation — grounded in trigonometric principles — when tracking and documenting animal movements in Amboseli or Tsavo.
UNIT.focus: This unit introduces Grade 10 learners to the foundational concepts of trigonometry through a hands-on, phenomenon-driven approach: learners begin by physically drawing right-angled triangles of their own chosen dimensions, measuring angles with a protractor, and computing the ratios of sides — discovering for themselves that these ratios depend only on the angle, not the size of the triangle. The unit progresses from defining sine, cosine, and tangent, through the use of mathematical tables and calculators, to elegant patterns in complementary and special angles, culminating in the powerful real-life applications of angles of elevation and depression in Kenyan contexts such as estimating the height of a water tower or a hill in the Rift Valley. Throughout, the storyline returns repeatedly to the anchoring phenomenon, with each new concept deepening students' ability to explain and model what they first observed.
UNIT.phenomenon:
ANCHORING PHENOMENON — "Why does my shadow tell me how tall the tree is?"

A student standing in the school compound in Kisumu on a sunny afternoon notices that her 1.6 m shadow is exactly 1.2 m long, while the shadow of a nearby mango tree is 9.0 m long. She wonders: can she figure out the height of the tree without climbing it? A classmate suggests they also measure the angle the sun's rays make with the ground using a simple clinometer made from a protractor and a straw. When they do, they get an angle of approximately 53°. The class is struck by the puzzle: how can an angle — something you measure with a protractor on paper — tell you the height of a real tree? This phenomenon is puzzling because it implies a hidden, consistent relationship between angles and the ratios of lengths in right-angled triangles, a relationship that seems to hold no matter how large or small the triangle. Students are asked to predict how this could be possible before any instruction begins, activating prior knowledge of ratio and similar triangles and generating genuine curiosity about trigonometric ratios.
UNIT.supportingPhenomena:
• SUPPORTING PHENOMENON 1 — "The leaning phone mast": Learners watch a short video of a telecommunications mast on a hillside near Kericho that leans at an angle. Engineers on site use only a tape measure and an angle-reading instrument to decide whether the mast is safe. Students are asked: what mathematical relationship allows two measurements to reveal a structural risk? This supports the introduction of sine and cosine of acute angles.
• SUPPORTING PHENOMENON 2 — "Reading the mountain": Learners examine a photograph taken from the Nairobi–Nakuru highway showing Mount Longonot. A road sign gives the horizontal distance to the crater rim as 4.2 km. Students measure the apparent angle of elevation from the photograph using a printed protractor overlay and calculate the crater height — connecting angles of elevation to real geography.
• SUPPORTING PHENOMENON 3 — "The tile cutter's 45°": A short article describes how a tile-fitter in Mombasa cutting square tiles diagonally always uses a 45° guide. Learners investigate why sin 45° = cos 45° and why the diagonal of a unit square is √2, naturally motivating the special-angles lesson.
• SUPPORTING PHENOMENON 4 — "Airport glide path": Learners read a brief excerpt explaining that aircraft approaching Wilson Airport in Nairobi follow a standard 3° glide-path angle of depression. They calculate how far from the runway threshold a plane is when it is 300 m above ground, making angles of depression concrete and consequential.
UNIT.drivingQuestion:
DRIVING QUESTION: How can the angle a sunbeam makes with the ground allow us to calculate the height of any tree, building, or hill in Kenya without measuring it directly?

KICD KEY INQUIRY QUESTIONS:
1. How do we apply trigonometric ratios in day-to-day activities?
2. How are the trigonometric ratios of acute angles related to each other?
UNIT.storylineThread:
Lesson 1 — Predict & Anchor Phenomenon: Students observe the mango-tree shadow scenario (anchoring phenomenon). In groups they predict how an angle could reveal a height, record predictions on sticky notes, and post them on the Driving Question Board (DQB). Teacher introduces the activity: each group draws three right-angled triangles of different sizes but with the same chosen acute angle, measures sides, and computes opposite/hypotenuse, adjacent/hypotenuse, and opposite/adjacent ratios — discovering these are constant. Sine, cosine, and tangent are formally defined.

Lesson 2 — Sine of an Acute Angle: Learners watch a short video on the sine of an acute angle. They use mathematical tables to read sine values for various angles and verify against their Lesson 1 triangle measurements. Teacher conducts cold-call questioning (minimum 6 students) on how to locate values in tables. Students annotate their DQB: "Sine depends only on the angle, not the triangle's size."

Lesson 3 — Cosine of an Acute Angle: Guided reading on cosine of an acute angle. Learners read cosine values from tables, compare with sine values, and begin noticing a complementary pattern. Pairs solve five structured problems finding unknown sides of right-angled triangles in Kenyan contexts (e.g., a ramp at a Nakuru hospital). DQB updated with new question: "Is there a link between sine and cosine?"

Lesson 4 — Tangent and the Sine–Cosine–Tangent Relationship: Learners derive tan θ = sin θ / cos θ algebraically and verify numerically using table values for 30°, 40°, 60°, 70°. Teacher facilitates a whole-class sensemaking discussion. Students revisit the mango-tree phenomenon and use tangent to calculate the tree's height from the 53° sun angle and the 9.0 m shadow. DQB updated: the driving question is partially answered for the first time.

Lesson 5 — Trigonometric Ratios Using Calculators: Learners use scientific calculators to find sine, cosine, and tangent values, and to find angles given a ratio (inverse functions). Activity: finding trigonometric ratios of acute angles using calculators with a structured worksheet. Students check their Lesson 1 hand-drawn triangle results against calculator values and compute percentage error, linking to integrity value. DQB updated with precision note.

Lesson 6 — Complementary Angles: Learners read an article and watch a short video on complementary angles. They discover sin θ = cos(90° − θ) through a table-comparison task, then prove it geometrically using a right-angled triangle diagram. Cold-call challenge: teacher asks 8 students to state a complementary-angle pair and write the corresponding identity. Supporting phenomenon (tile-cutter's 45°) is introduced to motivate the next lesson.

Lesson 7 — Special Angles (Derivation): Learners derive exact values for 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90° using two geometric constructions: an equilateral triangle (side 2) for 30°/60° and an isosceles right-angled triangle (legs of 1) for 45°. Video and reading resources on special angles are used to consolidate. Each group creates a "special angles passport card" — a pocket-sized reference they design themselves. DQB updated with exact-value table.

Lesson 8 — Special Angles (Application & Model Building): Learners use exact special-angle values (no calculator) to solve problems: e.g., a builder in Eldoret needs the length of a roof rafter for a 60° pitched roof of 5 m span. Reading and article on special angles applied to real structures. Groups revise their cumulative model (annotated right-angled triangle diagram) to include the special-angle values and the tan = sin/cos relationship.

Lesson 9 — Angles of Elevation and Depression: Learners watch a video and complete a reading on angles of elevation and depression. Using the airport glide-path supporting phenomenon, students calculate horizontal distances and heights. Outdoor mini-activity (if space allows): students use a clinometer (protractor + straw + plumb-line) to measure the angle of elevation to the top of the school's flagpole or water tank and calculate its height using a measured baseline distance. Results are compared across groups and discrepancies discussed.

Lesson 10 — Real-Life Applications, DQB Closure & Final Model Revision: Learners tackle an integrated problem set drawn from Kenyan contexts — surveying a plot boundary in Kisumu, calculating the height of a telecommunications mast in Kericho, and verifying the glide path at Wilson Airport. Groups present solutions. Full DQB review: every original prediction and question is revisited and resolved. Each learner submits a final annotated model diagram showing all unit concepts connected back to the anchoring mango-tree phenomenon, demonstrating how trigonometric ratios of acute angles answer the driving question.

LESSONS.title: Predict and Anchor: Why Does My Shadow Tell Me How Tall the Tree Is?
LESSONS.aresKeywords: trigonometric ratios, right-angled triangle, opposite side, adjacent side, hypotenuse, sine, cosine, tangent, angle, ratio, similar triangles

slo.purpose: To launch the sub-strand by anchoring learning in a real Kenyan phenomenon and generating genuine curiosity about the hidden relationship between angles and the ratios of sides in right-angled triangles.
slo.knowledge: Learners will recall properties of right-angled triangles and the concept of ratio from prior learning, and will be introduced to the idea that a fixed acute angle produces a constant ratio of sides regardless of the size of the triangle.
slo.skills: Learners will draw right-angled triangles of different sizes sharing the same acute angle, measure sides with a ruler, compute three ratios (opposite over hypotenuse, adjacent over hypotenuse, opposite over adjacent), and record results in a structured table.
slo.attitudes: Learners will demonstrate curiosity by making genuine predictions before instruction, and will show integrity by recording their measured values honestly even when results are unexpected.
slo.keyInquiry: How do we apply trigonometric ratios in day-to-day activities?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This is the opening lesson that launches the entire unit phenomenon. Learners encounter the mango-tree shadow puzzle from Kisumu, make unaided predictions, and then discover through hands-on triangle drawing that angle alone fixes the ratio of sides — the foundational insight that makes all subsequent lessons meaningful. The Driving Question Board is opened here and will be updated in every subsequent lesson until Lesson 10.
slo.safetyNotes: Ensure rulers and pencils are used safely during the drawing activity. If the lesson is taken outdoors to observe shadows, learners should not stare directly at the sun. No significant hazards are anticipated for an indoor lesson.

LESSONS.overview: The teacher presents the mango-tree shadow scenario set in Kisumu: a student of height 1.6 m casts a 1.2 m shadow while a nearby mango tree casts a 9.0 m shadow, and a classmate measures the sun-ray angle as approximately 53 degrees using a homemade clinometer. Learners first predict in groups how an angle measured with a protractor could reveal the height of a real tree, posting sticky-note predictions on the newly opened Driving Question Board. Groups then draw three right-angled triangles of different sizes but sharing one chosen acute angle, measure all three sides, and compute the three side ratios, discovering the ratios are constant across all triangle sizes. The teacher formally names these ratios sine, cosine, and tangent and connects them back to the phenomenon, leaving the full calculation for Lesson 4.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners sit in groups of four. The teacher reads aloud the mango-tree scenario slowly and deliberately, pausing to let learners visualise the compound in Kisumu, the student, her shadow, and the tree shadow. Each learner individually writes one prediction on a sticky note in response to the prompt: How could knowing only the angle of the sun help you find the height of the mango tree without climbing it? After two minutes of individual silent thinking, group members share their sticky notes with each other, discuss similarities and differences, and agree on one group prediction to post on the Driving Question Board. Individual sticky notes are also retained for personal portfolios. Predicted ideas typically include: the tree and student form similar triangles, the ratio of height to shadow stays the same, or the angle controls some number relationship.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher displays or sketches the scenario on the board: a stick figure of height 1.6 m with a shadow of 1.2 m beside a mango tree with a shadow of 9.0 m, labelling the sun-ray angle as 53 degrees. Teacher says: I want you to look at this picture carefully and just think. Do not calculate yet. On your sticky note, write your best prediction: how could the angle of those sun rays possibly tell us the height of the tree? You have two minutes of silence to think on your own. [WAIT TIME: 2 minutes of strict silence — teacher circulates without speaking, making eye contact and nodding to encourage.] After two minutes: Now share with your group. Choose the one prediction you all find most convincing. [WAIT TIME: 1.5 minutes for group share.] Teacher then cold-calls two groups to share their group prediction aloud, saying: Group two, share your sticky note with us. What is your group thinking? After each group responds, teacher says only: Interesting. Hold that idea. We are going to test it today. Teacher does not confirm or deny any prediction at this stage.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Predict-before-instruction activates prior knowledge of ratio and similar triangles from Grade 8 and 9 and surfaces alternative conceptions. The deliberate 2-minute silence ensures all learners engage individually before social influence occurs. Posting on the DQB externalises thinking and creates accountability — learners will return to these predictions in Lesson 4 and Lesson 10 to evaluate them.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans sticky notes during circulation to identify the range of prior knowledge: learners who mention similar triangles or ratio correctly, and learners who predict unrelated ideas such as measuring the shadow with a longer ruler. This informs the amount of scaffolding needed in the Explain Phase. The teacher makes a mental note of at least two groups to revisit during the Model Building Phase.

framework.learnerExperience: Each group receives a plain A4 sheet of paper, a ruler, a protractor, and a pencil. The teacher gives the instruction: draw three right-angled triangles — one small, one medium, one large — each containing the same acute angle. Groups choose their own angle (most will choose 30, 45, or 60 degrees because these are familiar from geometry, but any angle is allowed). Learners use the protractor to set the chosen angle at the base-left vertex, draw the base (adjacent side), erect a perpendicular (opposite side) using the right-angle corner, and connect the hypotenuse. The three triangles differ in base length but share the identical chosen angle. Learners label the right angle, the chosen angle (theta), the opposite side, the adjacent side, and the hypotenuse on each triangle, using the vocabulary from the board. They then measure all three sides of each triangle to the nearest millimetre and record the values in a prepared table with columns: triangle size, opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher models drawing one triangle on the board, narrating each step: I am placing my protractor at the bottom-left corner and marking 40 degrees. I draw my base along the bottom. At the right end of the base I draw a perpendicular line going straight up — that is my opposite side. Then I connect the top of that line back to my starting point — that is my hypotenuse. Teacher labels the sides clearly. Teacher then says: Now it is your turn. You choose your angle. Draw all three triangles on the same sheet of paper. Measure every side carefully. Record your values in the table. You have seven minutes. [WAIT TIME: 7 minutes — teacher circulates, checking that triangles genuinely share the same angle and that labelling is correct. Teacher corrects protractor misuse quietly at individual groups without whole-class interruption.] If a group finishes early, teacher prompts: Look at your three triangles. Do you notice anything interesting about the measurements?
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The hands-on drawing task grounds the abstract idea of angle in a physical, manipulable object. Drawing multiple sizes of the same triangle is the critical observational move — it sets up the cognitive conflict of the next phase, because learners will expect the sides to differ but will discover the ratios do not. Choosing their own angle gives learners agency and means different groups will have different angles, allowing cross-group comparison in the Explain Phase.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher observes whether learners correctly identify opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the chosen angle (not the right angle). A common error is labelling sides relative to the wrong vertex. Teacher notes any group making this error for targeted correction during the Explain Phase discussion.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners add three new columns to their table: opposite divided by hypotenuse, adjacent divided by hypotenuse, and opposite divided by adjacent. They compute each ratio as a decimal to two decimal places for all three triangles. Within groups, learners compare the three values in each ratio column and record what they notice. Groups are asked to write a one-sentence observation on the back of their A4 sheet. The teacher then facilitates a whole-class discussion, calling on groups that chose different angles to share their ratio values. The class discovers that each group has three nearly identical numbers in each ratio column, and that different groups have different ratio values because they chose different angles. The teacher formally names the ratios: opposite over hypotenuse is the sine of the angle, adjacent over hypotenuse is the cosine of the angle, and opposite over adjacent is the tangent of the angle. The teacher connects this immediately back to the Kisumu scenario: if the sun makes an angle of 53 degrees with the ground, then the tangent of 53 degrees equals the height of the tree divided by the length of the shadow.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher calls the class back to attention and asks: What do you notice about the three values in your first ratio column — opposite over hypotenuse? Cold-call to Group 1: Read out your three values. [WAIT TIME: 5 seconds for learner to respond.] Teacher writes the values on the board. Then to Group 3: Your group chose a different angle. Read out your three values for the same ratio. [WAIT TIME: 5 seconds.] Teacher writes these alongside. Teacher asks the class: These two groups chose different angles. Why are the values in each group almost the same, but different between the two groups? [WAIT TIME: 15 seconds of open silence before taking responses — this is a high-cognitive-demand question requiring genuine sensemaking.] Teacher accepts two or three learner responses without evaluating them yet, then says: The ratio depends only on the angle — not on how big the triangle is. That is the key idea of today. Let me give each ratio a name. Teacher writes on the board: sine theta equals opposite over hypotenuse, cosine theta equals adjacent over hypotenuse, tangent theta equals opposite over adjacent. Teacher says: Now look back at our tree in Kisumu. The sun ray, the tree, and the tree shadow form a right-angled triangle. The angle at the base is 53 degrees. The shadow is the adjacent side. The tree height is the opposite side. So tangent of 53 degrees equals height divided by 9.0 metres. We do not yet know the exact value of tangent 53 degrees — but we will by Lesson 4. Post on board: tan 53 degrees equals height divided by 9.0 m — TO BE SOLVED IN LESSON 4.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The cross-group comparison is a deliberate sensemaking move: seeing that different groups with the same angle get the same ratio, but different angles give different ratios, isolates the variable (angle) and the invariant (ratio for that angle). The formal naming of sine, cosine, and tangent is delayed until after learners have constructed the pattern themselves, ensuring the vocabulary labels a concept already partially understood. Explicitly leaving the tree height unsolved preserves the productive tension of the phenomenon across the unit.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher listens for whether learners can articulate that the ratio is constant because all three triangles are similar (same angles, proportional sides). If no group produces this explanation spontaneously, the teacher asks: What do we call triangles that have the same angles but different sizes? — connecting to Grade 9 similar triangles. Learners who can identify the correct sides relative to the labelled angle demonstrate readiness for Lesson 2; those who cannot are noted for targeted support.

framework.learnerExperience: The teacher directs attention to a dedicated section of the classroom wall or board reserved for the Driving Question Board, which will remain visible for all ten lessons. The large driving question is written at the top: How can the angle a sunbeam makes with the ground allow us to calculate the height of any tree, building, or hill in Kenya without measuring it directly? Each group brings their group prediction sticky note from the Predict Phase and places it on the DQB under the heading: Our Predictions. The teacher then invites every learner to write one question that arose during today's activity on a new sticky note and post it under the heading: Questions We Now Have. Learners circulate briefly to read each other's questions. The teacher reads several questions aloud, groups related ones together, and labels clusters. Typical emerging questions include: How do we find the exact value of sine or cosine for any angle? Is there a pattern between sine and cosine? Do these ratios work for angles bigger than 90 degrees? The teacher says: Every one of these questions will be answered during our ten lessons. Watch this board grow.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher says: This board belongs to you. Every prediction you made at the start is on it. Every question you have just posted is on it. At the end of every lesson you will add something new to it — something you have learned or a new question that opened up. By Lesson 10, you will come back to your own prediction from today and decide: were you right? Teacher physically attaches the group sticky notes, saying the group name aloud as each is placed, creating a sense of community ownership. Teacher does not remove or correct any prediction at this stage, including incorrect ones, explaining: We do not erase predictions — we revisit them with evidence later. Teacher writes the TO BE SOLVED note from the Explain Phase directly on the DQB near the driving question: tan 53 degrees times 9.0 m equals the height of the mango tree — we will calculate this in Lesson 4.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The DQB makes the intellectual journey of the unit visible and persistent. It activates metacognitive awareness — learners know they are at the beginning of an inquiry and that their own questions drive the sequence. Clustering questions publicly helps learners see the coherence of the sub-strand without the teacher having to impose it. Preserving incorrect predictions models scientific integrity — ideas are tested, not assumed.
framework.formativeAssessment: The teacher reads the posted questions during the brief circulation moment to gauge depth of curiosity and identify any misconceptions expressed as questions (for example, a learner might post: Does a bigger angle always give a bigger triangle? — revealing confusion between angle size and side length). These inform planning for Lesson 2 questioning sequences.

framework.learnerExperience: Each learner opens a dedicated Trigonometry Model Page — a blank or lightly gridded page at the front of their exercise book reserved for the evolving unit model. For Lesson 1, learners draw one clearly labelled right-angled triangle and annotate it with the three side names (opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse) relative to a labelled acute angle theta. They write the three ratio definitions: sin theta equals O over H, cos theta equals A over H, tan theta equals O over A. Below the triangle, they write one sentence connecting this to the Kisumu scenario: In the mango-tree problem, the angle of 53 degrees is theta, the shadow of 9.0 m is the adjacent side, and the tree height we want to find is the opposite side. Learners initial and date this page. The teacher reminds learners that this page will be revised and extended in every lesson until it becomes a complete model of the entire sub-strand by Lesson 10.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher says: At the back of your book, open a page labelled Trigonometry Model — Lesson 1. This page stays in your book for all ten lessons. Today, draw the triangle, label all the parts, write the three ratio definitions. Most importantly, write a sentence that connects the triangle to the mango tree. That sentence is the reason we are learning this. [WAIT TIME: 4 minutes for silent individual drawing and annotation.] Teacher circulates and spots-checks that side labels are relative to theta, not to the right angle. Teacher says to the class: In Lesson 2 we will discover exactly how to find the value of sine for any angle without drawing a triangle. Then in Lesson 4 we will finally calculate the height of that mango tree in Kisumu. Keep your predictions on the board — we are going to check them.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The model page is a living artefact of the learner's growing understanding, not a note-taking exercise. By starting it with the phenomenon connection required in the first entry, the teacher embeds the habit of always linking abstract content to the real-world anchor. The model page will be used in Lesson 10 as the final assessment artefact, giving it longitudinal value.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher collects one or two model pages briefly at the close of the lesson (or photographs them with a phone) to check correctness of labelling before Lesson 2. Exit criterion: learner can correctly label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse on a right-angled triangle relative to a specified acute angle, and can write all three ratio definitions. Learners who cannot meet this criterion receive brief individual feedback at the start of Lesson 2.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, consider these questions: Did at least 80 percent of groups independently notice that the ratios were constant across triangle sizes, or did most groups need direct prompting? Were the 15-second wait-time silences in the Explain Phase maintained, or did the teacher fill the silence too quickly? Did the DQB feel owned by the learners or did it feel like a teacher display? Were any learners unable to identify opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse correctly — and have they been noted for Lesson 2 support? Did the lesson end with genuine unresolved curiosity about the tree height, or did the session feel closed? The most critical indicator of success is whether learners leave wanting to know the value of tangent 53 degrees — if they do, the phenomenon has done its job.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: A student in Kisumu noticed that she could measure the angle of the sun with a clinometer and that both she and the mango tree cast shadows on the same sunny afternoon. Her shadow was 1.2 m and the tree shadow was 9.0 m, with a sun angle of approximately 53 degrees.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: When you draw right-angled triangles with the same acute angle but different sizes, the ratio of opposite to hypotenuse stays the same, the ratio of adjacent to hypotenuse stays the same, and the ratio of opposite to adjacent stays the same. These constant ratios are called sine, cosine, and tangent of that angle.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The ratios are constant because all right-angled triangles with the same acute angle are similar triangles — their sides are proportional. This means the angle alone determines the ratio, so if we know the angle and one side we can always find the other side. That is how the 53-degree sun angle can reveal the mango tree height from just the shadow length.

LESSONS.title: Sine of an Acute Angle: Reading the Language of Triangles

slo.purpose: To develop learners' understanding of the sine ratio as a consistent, angle-dependent relationship in right-angled triangles, and to build fluency in reading sine values from mathematical tables.
slo.knowledge: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) define the sine of an acute angle as the ratio of the opposite side to the hypotenuse in a right-angled triangle; (b) state that the sine ratio depends only on the angle, not on the size of the triangle; (c) read sine values for given acute angles from mathematical tables.
slo.skills: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) use mathematical tables accurately to look up sine values for acute angles; (b) verify sine values obtained from tables against measurements made on triangles drawn in Lesson 1; (c) apply the sine ratio to estimate heights in real contexts such as the mango tree shadow problem.
slo.attitudes: Learners appreciate the elegance and consistency of the sine ratio as a mathematical tool that works universally — from a small triangle drawn on paper to the height of a mango tree in Kisumu — and show responsibility in the careful, accurate use of mathematical tables.
slo.keyInquiry: How do we apply trigonometric ratios in day-to-day activities? How are the trigonometric ratios of acute angles related to each other?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 1 learners measured sides of right-angled triangles and computed the ratio opposite ÷ hypotenuse for several angles, noticing the ratio stayed the same regardless of triangle size. In Lesson 2 they discover that this consistent ratio has a name — sine — and that mathematicians have pre-computed it for every angle in mathematical tables. By reading those tables and matching the values to their own Lesson 1 measurements, learners gather crucial evidence that sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse is a universal, angle-only relationship. This advances the driving question: if sin 53° is always 0.7986, then once you know the sun makes a 53° angle with the ground, you can calculate the mango tree's height from nothing but the shadow length — no climbing required.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous materials are used in this lesson. Learners should handle mathematical tables carefully to avoid tearing. If the video is shown on a projected screen, ensure the room is not darkened to the point where learners cannot write notes safely.

LESSONS.overview: Learners begin by revisiting the anchoring phenomenon — the student in Kisumu and her mango-tree shadow — and are challenged to predict what the ratio opposite/hypotenuse for the 53° sun angle should be. They then watch a short focused video introducing the sine ratio and its notation. The central activity is a guided table-reading exercise in which learners use mathematical tables to find sine values for a set of angles (including those measured in Lesson 1), then compare table values to their own calculated ratios from the previous lesson. The teacher conducts structured cold-call questioning across at least 6 students to build fluency with table navigation. Learners close by annotating the Driving Question Board with the key insight: "Sine depends only on the angle, not the triangle's size," and revising their cumulative models to incorporate the sine ratio.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown the Kisumu shadow scenario on the board: a girl of height 1.6 m casts a shadow of 1.2 m; a mango tree casts a shadow of 9.0 m; the sun's rays make an angle of 53° with the ground. Each learner individually writes a prediction in their exercise books: 'What is the value of opposite ÷ hypotenuse for the sun-ray triangle at 53°? Will this value change if the mango tree were taller or shorter?' Learners also predict whether the ratio they calculated in Lesson 1 for their drawn triangles will match a value they can look up somewhere. They then share predictions with a shoulder partner (Think-Pair) before the teacher takes 3 cold-call responses to surface a range of ideas — including likely misconceptions that the ratio changes with triangle size.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the phenomenon diagram (sun ray, tree, shadow forming a right-angled triangle labelled with O = tree height, H = sun-ray length, angle = 53°). Say verbatim: 'Look at this triangle. In your book, write your best estimate of the value of opposite divided by hypotenuse for a 53° angle. You have 90 seconds — work silently.' [Wait 90 seconds.] 'Now share with your partner for 30 seconds.' [Wait 30 seconds.] Cold-call Student 1: 'Achieng, what did you predict and why?' [Wait 10 seconds for response.] Cold-call Student 2: 'Otieno, do you agree with Achieng? What would change if the tree were twice as tall?' [Wait 12 seconds.] Cold-call Student 3: 'Wanjiru, if the angle stayed at 53° but the shadow was 18 m long instead of 9 m, would your ratio change?' [Wait 10 seconds.] Record all three predictions on the board under the heading 'Our Predictions — we will check these later.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Think-Pair-Share with recorded public predictions. This strategy externalises prior knowledge and creates cognitive dissonance when learners later discover the ratio is constant — making the sine concept genuinely surprising and memorable. Recording predictions publicly creates accountability and a reference point for revision at the end of the lesson.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Every learner has a written numerical prediction in their exercise book before sharing begins. The teacher scans books during the 90-second window. Misconception to listen for: 'The ratio gets bigger when the triangle gets bigger' — flag this without correcting yet, saying: 'Let's keep that idea — we will test it today.'

framework.learnerExperience: Learners watch a 6–8 minute video introducing the sine ratio: its definition (sin θ = Opposite/Hypotenuse), its notation, and a worked example showing that two right-angled triangles with the same 40° angle but different sizes yield the same sine value (approximately 0.6428). The video includes a Kenyan context — a surveyor in the Rift Valley measuring the angle of elevation to a hill. After the video, learners immediately open their mathematical tables to the sine pages. Working in pairs, they complete a structured table-reading activity: they are given a list of 8 angles (20°, 30°, 37°, 45°, 53°, 60°, 72°, 90°) and must record the sine value from the tables for each. For 53° and one angle of their choice from Lesson 1, they also retrieve the ratio they computed from their own drawn triangles and write both values side by side for comparison.
framework.teacherMoves: Before playing the video say: 'As you watch, write down: (1) the definition of sine, (2) one thing that surprises you.' Play the video. After the video, give the instruction: 'Open your mathematical tables to the sine section. I will give you 4 minutes to find all 8 sine values on your worksheet. Start now.' [Circulate, observe table-navigation strategies.] After 4 minutes, begin cold-call table-reading drill — minimum 6 students: Cold-call Student 4: 'Kamau, what is sin 20°? Tell me exactly which column and row you used.' [Wait 10 seconds.] Cold-call Student 5: 'Nafula, what is sin 37°? Did anyone get a different value?' [Wait 10 seconds.] Cold-call Student 6: 'Mwangi, what is sin 53°? Now look at the ratio you measured for your 53° triangle in Lesson 1 — what do you notice?' [Wait 12 seconds.] Cold-call Student 7: 'Asha, what is sin 90° and does that surprise you? Why?' [Wait 10 seconds.] Cold-call Student 8: 'Omondi, what is sin 45°? Can you express that as a fraction or a decimal?' [Wait 10 seconds.] Cold-call Student 9: 'Chebet, if I gave you sin 60°, could you immediately tell me which side would be the longest — opposite or adjacent? Explain.' [Wait 15 seconds.] Summarise on the board: write each angle and its sine value as learners confirm answers. Draw attention to sin 53° ≈ 0.7986 and link back to the shadow scenario.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Data-to-concept bridging: Learners collect two parallel data sets — table-derived sine values and their own Lesson 1 measured ratios — and place them side by side. This concrete comparison makes the abstraction (sine is a universal constant for a given angle) visible and personally verifiable rather than teacher-asserted. The structured cold-call drill on table navigation builds procedural fluency through spaced retrieval.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Each pair's worksheet shows 8 correctly located sine values with the column/row reference noted. (2) For sin 53°, learners can articulate that their Lesson 1 measured ratio (≈ 0.80) matches the table value (0.7986) within measurement error. Listen for: any learner who says 'my ratio was different' — use this as a teachable moment about measurement precision, not as an error to dismiss.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners work in groups of four to complete a sensemaking task: they are given three right-angled triangles of different sizes, all with a 37° angle, with the opposite and hypotenuse sides labelled in centimetres (small: 3 cm and 5 cm; medium: 6 cm and 10 cm; large: 9 cm and 15 cm). They calculate opposite/hypotenuse for each and compare to sin 37° from their tables. They then write a group explanation in their own words: 'Why does the ratio stay the same even as the triangle grows?' Groups share their explanations; the teacher guides learners to connect this to the concept of similar triangles (linked to prior learning). The teacher then models applying sin 53° = 0.7986 to solve the mango tree problem: if the shadow is 9.0 m and the angle of elevation is 53°, what is the hypotenuse (sun-ray length)? From that, what is the tree height? Learners solve a parallel problem individually: a mobile phone mast in Nakuru casts a shadow of 12 m when the sun's elevation angle is 40°. Find the height of the mast using sin 40° from tables.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute the three-triangle handout and say: 'In your group, compute opposite divided by hypotenuse for each triangle and compare to your table value for sin 37°. You have 5 minutes. Nominate a scribe and a speaker.' [Circulate, prompt groups with: 'What do you notice about your three ratios? Can you explain WHY they are the same using what you know about similar triangles?'] After 5 minutes, take group reports: 'Group 2 — what did you find? Use the word SIMILAR in your explanation.' [Wait 10 seconds.] Affirm correct use of similar triangles language. Then model the mango tree calculation on the board step-by-step: 'sin 53° = opposite / hypotenuse → 0.7986 = tree height / sun-ray length. But wait — we know the shadow, not the sun-ray. Which ratio connects opposite and adjacent? We will explore that in Lesson 3. For now, let's use what we do know.' Guide learners to set up: shadow/hypotenuse = cos 53° [preview only — do not teach cosine yet]. Then pivot to the Nakuru mast problem and circulate while learners solve individually. Cold-call Student 10: 'Kiptoo, walk us through your setup for the Nakuru mast. What did you write as your sine equation?' [Wait 15 seconds.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Analogical reasoning with scaling: by computing the ratio for three triangles that are scalar multiples of each other (3-5, 6-10, 9-15), learners see that scaling preserves angles and ratios — connecting sine to the geometry of similar triangles they already know. This makes the 'why' of sine visible, not just the 'what'. The individual problem application immediately transfers understanding to a new Kenyan context.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Every group's written explanation includes a reference to 'similar triangles' or 'same shape, different size.' (2) In individual work, at least 80% of learners correctly write the sine equation (sin 40° = height / hypotenuse) before attempting to solve. (3) Kiptoo's cold-call response reveals whether learners can set up — not just solve — the equation. Misconception to watch for: learners multiplying angle × side length instead of using the ratio.

framework.learnerExperience: Each learner receives a sticky note (or writes in a designated DQB section of their book if physical sticky notes are unavailable). They respond to the prompt: 'What new evidence have you gathered today that helps answer our driving question: How can an angle tell us the height of a tree?' They also add a second note under the sub-heading 'New Questions I Have Now' — learners often spontaneously ask about what happens with the shadow length (cosine) or how tables were originally calculated. The class DQB is updated collectively: the teacher reads out 3–4 sticky notes, clusters them, and adds the lesson's anchor insight in a distinct colour: 'sin θ = O/H and depends ONLY on θ — not on the triangle's size.' The teacher explicitly links this to the Kisumu phenomenon: 'So now we know — sin 53° is always 0.7986. The sun doesn't change the angle just because the tree is taller. This means we are one step closer to calculating that mango tree's height.'
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute sticky notes and say: 'You have 3 minutes. On your note, write one piece of evidence from today that moves us closer to answering our driving question. Be specific — use numbers if you can.' [Wait 3 minutes, circulate.] Collect notes. Read 3 aloud, paraphrasing: 'This learner says sin 53° = 0.7986 and it matched what they measured — that is strong evidence.' Place notes on the board. Ask the class: 'What single sentence captures what we learned today about sine?' Take 2 cold-call responses, then write the class-agreed anchor insight. Point to the driving question and say: 'What do we still NOT know that we need to answer the full question?' Accept 2–3 responses and add them to the 'Still Wondering' column of the DQB.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Public knowledge aggregation on the DQB: making individual insights collective and visible builds a shared scientific narrative. The 'Still Wondering' column sustains inquiry momentum and gives the teacher real-time data on what gaps remain before Lesson 3 (cosine).
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Every learner's sticky note contains a specific, evidence-based claim (not a vague statement like 'I learned sine'). (2) The class-agreed anchor insight correctly states that sine depends only on the angle. (3) 'Still Wondering' questions reveal whether learners are ready for cosine and tangent — the teacher notes these for Lesson 3 planning.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners retrieve their cumulative model from Lesson 1 — a labelled right-angled triangle showing the phenomenon (sun ray, tree, shadow). They now add the sine relationship to their model: they annotate the hypotenuse and opposite side, write 'sin θ = O/H' inside the triangle, and add the specific value 'sin 53° ≈ 0.7986' with an arrow linking it to the mango tree scenario. They also add a 'model note box' alongside the triangle with the statement: 'This ratio is fixed for any given angle — it does not change when the triangle gets bigger or smaller (similar triangles).' Pairs peer-review each other's models against a simple 3-item checklist: (1) Is the opposite side correctly identified relative to the 53° angle? (2) Is sin θ = O/H written correctly? (3) Is the 'angle-only' insight recorded? Learners make any corrections in a different colour pen.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Take out your Lesson 1 model. Today we are going to upgrade it with what we now know about sine. You have 6 minutes to annotate your model — add labels, the formula, and the specific value for 53°. Your model should tell the story of why you can calculate the mango tree's height.' [Circulate, prompt: 'Where is your opposite side relative to the 53° angle?' and 'Have you written what stays constant?'] After 6 minutes: 'Now swap with your partner and use the checklist on the board to review their model. Give them one specific, positive comment and one specific suggestion.' [Wait 4 minutes for peer review.] Close by saying: 'Your model is growing. By Lesson 10 it will contain everything needed to answer our driving question completely. What did you add today?' Take 2 final cold-call responses.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Iterative model revision: learners physically modify a persistent artefact across lessons, making their evolving understanding visible and cumulative. The peer-review checklist introduces disciplined critique — a key science and engineering practice — while keeping the focus on the phenomenon rather than abstract correctness.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) The opposite side in every model is correctly labelled relative to the stated angle (not the right angle or the other acute angle). (2) The formula sin θ = O/H appears on all models. (3) The 'angle-only' insight is recorded in the model note box. (4) Peer-review checklists are completed with specific (not generic) feedback. Models not meeting criterion (1) are flagged for teacher follow-up at the start of Lesson 3.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) During the cold-call table-reading drill, which learners struggled to navigate the sine tables — did they have difficulty with rows vs. columns, or with decimal place value? Plan a 3-minute re-teaching moment at the start of Lesson 3 if more than 3 learners showed this difficulty. (2) Did learners' explanations in Phase 3 explicitly reference similar triangles, or did they simply state the ratio 'happened to be the same'? If the similar-triangles connection was weak, consider adding a brief visual proof in Lesson 3's opening. (3) Review the 'Still Wondering' sticky notes from the DQB: are learners asking about the shadow-to-height ratio (which requires cosine/tangent)? Use their own questions as the opening hook for Lesson 3. (4) Were the Lesson 1 measured ratios close enough to the sine table values to build confidence? If many learners found large discrepancies, this is a measurement-precision issue — address it by discussing significant figures before Lesson 3's cosine work. (5) Did the Nakuru mast individual problem reveal any systematic errors in equation setup? Collect and review at least 10 learners' books before Lesson 3.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners watched a video showing that two right-angled triangles with the same 40° angle but different sizes produce the same value of opposite ÷ hypotenuse. They also observed that a surveyor in the Rift Valley uses this relationship to measure hill heights without direct measurement.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: The sine of an acute angle (sin θ) is defined as opposite ÷ hypotenuse in a right-angled triangle and is a fixed value that depends only on the angle — not on the size of the triangle. For the mango tree scenario, sin 53° ≈ 0.7986, a value that can be read directly from mathematical tables.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Learners explained that because triangles with the same angle are similar (same shape, scaled size), their side ratios are always equal — which is why sin 53° is the same whether the triangle is drawn on paper or formed by a sun ray and a 20-metre tree in Kisumu. This means knowing just the angle and one side length is enough to find any other side — bringing us one step closer to calculating the mango tree's height without climbing it.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: sine ratio, sin theta, opposite over hypotenuse, SOH, mathematical tables, acute angles, right-angled triangle, similar triangles, angle of elevation, trigonometric ratios, shadow problem, Kisumu, mango tree, Nakuru, clinometer, table reading, sine values, Grade 10 mathematics, Trigonometry I, CBE Kenya

LESSONS.title: Cosine of an Acute Angle: The Adjacent Side Speaks
LESSONS.aresKeywords: cosine of an acute angle, adjacent side, hypotenuse, trigonometric ratio, mathematical tables, right-angled triangle, complementary pattern

slo.purpose: By the end of this lesson, learners will understand that the cosine of an acute angle is a fixed ratio of the adjacent side to the hypotenuse, read cosine values from mathematical tables, and use cosine to find unknown sides of right-angled triangles in real Kenyan contexts.
slo.knowledge: The cosine of an acute angle theta is defined as cos theta = adjacent divided by hypotenuse; cosine values depend only on the angle and not on the size of the triangle; cosine values can be read from mathematical tables and decrease as the angle increases from 0 degrees to 90 degrees.
slo.skills: Reading cosine values from mathematical tables for given angles; identifying the adjacent side and hypotenuse in a right-angled triangle for a specified angle; using cos theta to calculate unknown sides; comparing sine and cosine values to begin detecting a complementary pattern.
slo.attitudes: Curiosity about the hidden relationship between sine and cosine; diligence in careful table reading; collaborative respect when working in pairs on structured problems.
slo.keyInquiry: How are the trigonometric ratios of acute angles related to each other? — specifically, how does cosine compare with the sine values already discovered, and what pattern begins to emerge?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 2 learners established that sine captures the ratio involving the side opposite the angle and the hypotenuse. Lesson 3 introduces cosine, the ratio involving the adjacent side, giving learners a second measurement tool. Together sine and cosine will allow them to approach the mango-tree problem from multiple angles (literally) and set up the crucial relationship tan theta = sin theta divided by cos theta to be derived in Lesson 4.
slo.safetyNotes: No physical hazards. Remind learners to handle mathematical tables booklets with care to avoid tearing; rulers used for underlining table rows should be kept flat on desks.

LESSONS.overview: Learners begin with a guided reading excerpt about the cosine ratio in the context of a wheelchair ramp at a Nakuru hospital. They predict what cos 53 degrees will be relative to sin 53 degrees by sketching the mango-tree right-angled triangle and labelling adjacent and opposite sides. Through a structured table-reading activity they discover cosine values for a range of angles, compare them with sine values, and notice that cos theta for a given angle equals sin of the complementary angle — a pattern they record as a question without yet proving it. Pairs then solve five structured problems involving Kenyan contexts. The Driving Question Board is updated with a new question: Is there a link between sine and cosine?

framework.learnerExperience: Learners open their exercise books to the right-angled triangle diagram of the mango-tree scenario drawn in Lesson 1. The teacher reminds them that in Lesson 2 they used the side opposite 53 degrees (height of tree) and the hypotenuse (length of sun ray). The teacher now points to the remaining labelled side — the 9.0 m shadow on the ground — and asks: what ratio would use THAT side and the hypotenuse? Each learner individually writes a prediction in their book: (a) what they think this new ratio will be called, (b) whether they expect its value to be larger or smaller than sin 53 degrees (approximately 0.7986), and (c) why. After 2 minutes of silent individual writing, learners share predictions with their seat partner for 1 minute.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher holds up the mango-tree diagram on the board (drawn or projected) and traces the 9.0 m shadow with a finger, saying: In Lesson 2 we listened to the opposite side — it gave us sine. Today we listen to THIS side, the one that runs along the ground next to the angle. Without telling me the answer, write down what you predict: is the ratio of this ground-shadow side to the sun-ray hypotenuse going to be bigger than 0.7986, smaller, or the same — and why? WAIT 90 seconds of complete silence. Then: Share your prediction with the person next to you. You have 60 seconds. After sharing, cold-call two pairs using their names: ask one learner to give the prediction and the partner to give the reason.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Anchoring the new ratio to the already-familiar mango-tree triangle gives cosine an immediate physical meaning. Requiring both a numerical prediction and a reason forces learners to reason from the geometry (the adjacent side is shorter than the hypotenuse, so the ratio must be less than 1; comparing it with sine requires thinking about which side is longer — opposite or adjacent — in a 53-degree triangle, where opposite is longer than adjacent, so cosine should be less than sine).
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher listens to the two cold-called pairs and notes on a clipboard whether learners (a) correctly identify that the ratio must be between 0 and 1 and (b) reason about whether it will be less than or greater than sin 53 degrees. This reveals whether learners have a geometric sense of the triangle rather than just memorised the sine formula.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners complete a guided reading of a one-page excerpt (prepared by the teacher or taken from the KICD-approved textbook) about a ramp built at a Nakuru County Referral Hospital to allow wheelchair access. The ramp rises 0.9 m over a horizontal distance of 1.2 m and the ramp surface (hypotenuse) is 1.5 m. The excerpt defines the cosine of the angle of inclination as adjacent divided by hypotenuse, i.e., 1.2 divided by 1.5 = 0.8, and names the angle theta. After reading, learners open mathematical tables to the cosine pages and complete a structured table-reading worksheet: they find cos values for 10, 20, 30, 37, 45, 53, 60, 70, 80 degrees and record them alongside the sine values they already recorded in Lesson 2. They then answer two observation questions on the worksheet: (1) What happens to cosine as the angle increases? (2) For which angle is cos theta equal to sin theta?
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher circulates and reads the excerpt aloud once with the class to model fluent reading of mathematical text. Then says: Now open your mathematical tables to the cosine section. The layout is the same as the sine section — I want complete silence for 5 minutes while you find each value and write it in your table. WAIT 5 minutes, circulating to check table-reading technique. If a learner is struggling, teacher points to the correct row and column silently. After 5 minutes: Compare your cosine column with your sine column. Answer the two observation questions on your worksheet. You have 3 minutes. WAIT 3 minutes. Cold-call three learners: What did you notice as the angle gets bigger? What angle makes sine and cosine equal? Why might that be?
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Placing cosine values directly beside sine values in the same table is a deliberate side-by-side comparison strategy. Learners are not told about the complementary relationship — they discover that cos 53 approximately equals sin 37, cos 30 approximately equals sin 60, etc. The Nakuru hospital ramp provides a concrete low-technology context that is familiar (disability access ramps are present in many Kenyan public buildings) and makes the adjacent-over-hypotenuse definition tangible.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans completed worksheets during circulation. Key checkpoints: Is cos listed as decreasing (from close to 1 at small angles to close to 0 at large angles)? Has the learner identified 45 degrees as the angle where sine equals cosine? Misconception to flag: some learners may read the wrong column in the cosine table (confusing mean-difference columns); teacher corrects silently by pointing to the column headers.

framework.learnerExperience: Teacher formally defines cosine and then sets pairs to work on five structured problems. Problem contexts: (1) A wheelchair ramp at Nakuru County Referral Hospital makes an angle of 12 degrees with the ground. The ramp surface is 4.0 m long. Find the horizontal distance (cos 12 degrees times 4.0). (2) A telecommunications cable runs from the top of a 10 m vertical pole in Kisumu to a point on the ground, making an angle of 40 degrees with the ground. Find the length of the cable (the hypotenuse) using the adjacent side (10 m) and cos 40 degrees — learners must recognise that 10 m is the adjacent side to the 40-degree angle at the base. Wait — teacher pauses class to confirm the triangle orientation before continuing. (3) A solar panel on a roof in Eldoret is tilted at 25 degrees to the horizontal. If the panel is 2.4 m long (hypotenuse), find the horizontal width of the panel on the roof (adjacent). (4) A footpath in Kakamega Forest runs at 35 degrees to the horizontal for 500 m. Find the horizontal displacement. (5) A ladybird walks along a ramp of length 30 cm inclined at 48 degrees. How far does it travel horizontally? For each problem, pairs must: draw and label the right-angled triangle, identify adjacent and hypotenuse, write the cosine equation, substitute table values, and solve.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher writes on the board: cos theta = adjacent divided by hypotenuse, and says: Cosine is the ratio of the side that RUNS ALONGSIDE the angle — the adjacent side — to the hypotenuse. It is constant for a given angle regardless of triangle size, exactly as sine was. Now with your partner, tackle the five problems on your worksheet. Draw the triangle FIRST — do not write an equation until you have drawn and labelled. WAIT 10 minutes for pair work, circulating actively. At the 5-minute mark, pause the class: Cold-call one pair to draw Problem 2 triangle on the board. Ask the class: Is the 10 m side the opposite or adjacent to the 40-degree angle? WAIT 20 seconds. Resolve any disagreement. Resume pair work for 5 more minutes. Teacher selects one pair to present Problem 4 solution to the class and facilitates a 2-minute whole-class check.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Requiring learners to draw and label the triangle before writing equations is a sensemaking protocol that prevents ratio-swapping errors (the most common mistake at this stage). The deliberate mid-activity pause on Problem 2 addresses a known difficulty: when the vertical side is given and the angle is at the base, learners often misidentify adjacent and opposite. Presenting a Kenyan forest footpath, a solar panel, and a hospital ramp in the same lesson emphasises that cosine is a universal tool used in everyday Kenyan infrastructure.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher uses a clipboard checklist: for each pair, note whether (a) triangles are drawn and correctly labelled before equations are written, (b) the cosine equation is correctly set up (cos theta = adj/hyp), (c) table values are read correctly to 4 decimal places, and (d) arithmetic is carried through correctly. Common error to watch for: inverting the equation to hyp = adj divided by cos theta — teacher asks the pair to re-examine their triangle and confirm which quantity is unknown.

framework.learnerExperience: Each pair writes one new sticky note to add to the class Driving Question Board. The sticky note must contain: (a) a statement of what cosine measures in the mango-tree scenario (the ratio of the 9.0 m shadow to the sun-ray length), and (b) the new question arising from their observation table: Is there a link between sine and cosine? Why does cos 53 degrees look similar to sin 37 degrees? Pairs post their sticky notes in a new column on the DQB labelled Cosine Evidence. The teacher then reads aloud the cumulative DQB narrative: In Lesson 1 we found that ratios in right-angled triangles are constant for a given angle. In Lesson 2 sine gave us a ratio using the opposite side. Today cosine gives us the adjacent side ratio. Something strange is appearing in our tables. That strange pattern is where we are heading next.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher distributes sticky notes (yellow for new questions, green for new evidence — a colour code established in Lesson 1). Says: Write your cosine statement in green and your new question in yellow. You have 2 minutes. WAIT 2 minutes. Invite two pairs to read their sticky notes before posting. After posting, teacher stands at the DQB and explicitly connects today to the storyline: Our shadow question is getting closer to an answer. We now have TWO ratios — sine uses opposite and hypotenuse, cosine uses adjacent and hypotenuse. In Lesson 4 we will find the third ratio — tangent — and we will discover it connects sine and cosine in a beautiful way that will finally let us calculate the mango-tree height directly from the sun-angle alone.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The two-colour sticky-note system makes the epistemological distinction between evidence (what we observed) and questions (what we still wonder) visible on the board. Narrating the cumulative storyline aloud reinforces that each lesson is a step in a coherent investigation, not isolated content, and primes learners for the tangent derivation in Lesson 4.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads all posted sticky notes after class. Quality indicator: the green evidence note should correctly state that cosine equals adjacent divided by hypotenuse and should give a specific numerical example from the lesson (e.g., cos 53 degrees is approximately 0.6018 in the mango-tree triangle). If sticky notes are vague or incorrect, teacher uses the first 2 minutes of Lesson 4 to clarify.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the cumulative annotated right-angled triangle diagram begun in Lesson 1 and extended in Lesson 2. In their exercise books they add the following to the mango-tree triangle diagram: (1) A label on the adjacent side (9.0 m shadow) with the notation adj. (2) The cosine definition box: cos theta = adj divided by hyp, with the calculated value cos 53 degrees approximately 0.6018. (3) A two-column comparison table (sine column from Lesson 2, cosine column from today) for angles 30, 45, 53, 60. (4) An arrow and question mark connecting the sin 53 degrees row to the cos 37 degrees row with the note: pattern to investigate. They also write one sentence completing the stem: In the mango-tree triangle, cosine tells us... Learners who finish early calculate the length of the sun-ray hypotenuse using cos 53 degrees and the 9.0 m adjacent shadow length, checking it against the value obtained from sine in Lesson 2.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher says: Update your triangle diagram now. Add the adjacent label, the cosine definition box, the comparison table, and your pattern arrow. You have 4 minutes. WAIT 4 minutes. Circulate and stamp or initial three to five diagrams as a spot-check. Say to the class: Notice that your diagram now has two definition boxes — sine and cosine. Next lesson we add a third, and the three together will crack the mango-tree problem open completely. For those who finish early: use cos 53 degrees and the 9.0 m shadow to find the sun-ray length. Does that length match what you calculated using sine in Lesson 2? If it does not, find the error.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Updating the same cumulative diagram each lesson builds a physical artefact of the learners knowledge growth. Asking learners to use cosine to find the sun-ray length and cross-check with the sine result from Lesson 2 is a coherence check — both approaches must give the same hypotenuse, reinforcing that sine and cosine are complementary tools describing the same triangle. This foreshadows the Pythagorean and complementary relationships to be formalised in Lessons 6 and 7.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher collects or photographs two or three diagrams to review after class. Criteria: (a) adjacent side correctly labelled on the 9.0 m shadow leg, (b) cosine definition box correctly written, (c) comparison table populated with reasonable 4-decimal values from tables, (d) pattern arrow present. The extension calculation should yield hypotenuse approximately equal to 9.0 divided by 0.6018 which is approximately 14.96 m, consistent with 1.6 divided by sin 53 degrees from Lesson 2 (approximately 14.96 m if the tree height was calculated as approximately 11.95 m and used as opposite). Teacher notes any discrepancy as a discussion seed for Lesson 4.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) Did learners correctly distinguish adjacent from opposite when setting up cosine equations, or was there persistent confusion especially in non-standard triangle orientations such as Problem 2? Note names of learners who need a brief individual conversation at the start of Lesson 4. (2) Did the side-by-side sine-cosine table reveal the complementary pattern to learners spontaneously, or did they need explicit prompting? If prompting was needed, consider whether Lesson 6 on complementary angles needs an additional bridging activity. (3) Were all five structured problems attempted by most pairs within 10 minutes, or was the pace too fast? If more than one third of pairs did not finish Problem 4 or 5, reduce the extension problem set in Lesson 4 warm-up accordingly. (4) Check the DQB sticky notes: do learners show genuine curiosity about the sine-cosine pattern, or are their questions superficial? Genuine curiosity notes indicate readiness for the tangent derivation; superficial notes suggest the pattern was not noticed and needs revisiting in the first 3 minutes of Lesson 4.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners observed that the cosine of an acute angle is a fixed ratio (adjacent divided by hypotenuse) that can be read from mathematical tables, that cosine values decrease as the angle increases, and that for each angle in their comparison table the cosine value appeared to match the sine value of a different angle — specifically the angle that adds up to 90 degrees.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: The cosine of an acute angle theta equals the length of the adjacent side divided by the length of the hypotenuse of any right-angled triangle containing theta. Like sine, this value depends only on the angle and not on the size of the triangle. For the mango-tree scenario, cos 53 degrees is approximately 0.6018, meaning the 9.0 m shadow is approximately 0.6018 times the length of the sun-ray reaching the tree tip.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Cosine, like sine, is a consistent fingerprint of an angle: it compresses the shape of any right-angled triangle with that angle into a single number. Because every right-angled triangle with a 53-degree angle has the same ratio of adjacent to hypotenuse regardless of size, a table entry is sufficient to solve for any unknown side in any such triangle anywhere in Kenya — from a hospital ramp in Nakuru to the shadow of the mango tree in Kisumu.

LESSONS.title: Tangent and the Sine–Cosine–Tangent Relationship: Solving the Mango-Tree Problem

slo.purpose: To derive the identity tan θ = sin θ / cos θ algebraically, verify it numerically using trigonometric table values, and apply the tangent ratio to calculate the height of the mango tree from the anchoring phenomenon.
slo.knowledge: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) define the tangent ratio as Opposite/Adjacent in a right-angled triangle; (b) derive the identity tan θ = sin θ / cos θ algebraically using the definitions of sine and cosine; (c) verify the identity numerically for θ = 30°, 40°, 60°, and 70° using mathematical tables; (d) apply the tangent ratio to calculate unknown heights and distances in real-life right-triangle problems.
slo.skills: Algebraic manipulation and symbolic reasoning; reading and interpreting trigonometric tables; numerical verification of a mathematical identity; applying SOH-CAH-TOA to a real-world measurement problem; constructing a logical mathematical argument.
slo.attitudes: Appreciate that mathematical identities reveal deep, consistent structure in nature; show curiosity about how a single angle measurement can unlock unknown lengths; demonstrate persistence when verifying results numerically; value collaborative sense-making in mathematics.
slo.keyInquiry: How do we apply trigonometric ratios in day-to-day activities? How are the trigonometric ratios of acute angles related to each other?
slo.purposeInStoryline: Lesson 4 is the pivotal evidence-gathering lesson in which the driving question is partially answered for the first time. Having established sin and cos in Lessons 1–3, learners now derive tan θ = sin θ / cos θ, see it confirmed numerically across four angles, and — crucially — use tan 53° with the 9.0 m shadow length to calculate the mango tree's height. The DQB is updated to mark this breakthrough: the class now has a working method for measuring any height using only an angle and a shadow.
slo.safetyNotes: No physical hazards in this lesson. If learners use printed mathematical tables, remind them to handle them carefully and not to write on shared copies. If a clinometer or protractor is passed around, ensure it is handled gently to avoid damage.

LESSONS.overview: Learners begin by revisiting their earlier predictions about the mango-tree phenomenon and recalling the sin and cos definitions established in Lessons 1–3. Through guided algebraic derivation, they prove that tan θ = sin θ / cos θ. Working in pairs, they then verify this identity numerically for 30°, 40°, 60°, and 70° using mathematical tables, building confidence in the result. A whole-class sensemaking discussion consolidates understanding of why all three ratios are interconnected. The lesson reaches its climax when learners apply tan 53° and the 9.0 m shadow length to calculate the mango tree's height — the first direct, quantitative answer to the driving question. The lesson closes with a DQB update where learners record what has now been explained and what questions remain (e.g., what happens when we know the height but not the angle?).

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown the anchoring phenomenon scenario on the board: 'A student in Kisumu measures the sun angle as 53° and the mango tree's shadow as 9.0 m. She already knows sin 53° ≈ 0.799 and cos 53° ≈ 0.602 from the previous lesson. Can she calculate the tree's height — and if so, which ratio will she need?' Each learner writes a silent prediction in their exercise book (2 minutes): (a) which trigonometric ratio they think is most useful and why; (b) a numerical estimate of the tree's height. Learners then share predictions in a think-pair-share with their desk partner before three pairs are cold-called to share with the class. Predictions are pinned to the Driving Question Board under the heading 'Our Predictions — Lesson 4' without correction.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the scenario on the board and read it aloud. Say: 'We have been gathering evidence about sin and cos for three lessons. Today we are going to find out whether there is a third ratio hiding in the triangle — one that connects the angle directly to the tree's height and shadow. But first, I want to know what YOU think.' Instruct learners to write silently for 2 minutes — no talking. After 2 minutes say: 'Now turn to your partner and compare your prediction. You have 90 seconds.' After sharing, cold-call exactly 3 pairs (choose a mix of confident and hesitant learners): 'Pair at the window bench — what ratio did you predict and why?' Allow a WAIT TIME of 12 seconds after each question before prompting. Record key words from each response on the board (e.g., 'Opposite/Adjacent', 'sin/cos', 'SOH-CAH-TOA'). Do NOT confirm or deny any prediction. Say: 'Hold those ideas — by the end of the lesson you will be able to judge your own prediction.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Think-Pair-Share with public prediction display. This activates prior knowledge of SOH-CAH-TOA and similar triangles from Lessons 1–3, creates cognitive investment in the outcome, and gives the teacher immediate formative data about whether learners are connecting ratio definitions to the physical scenario.
