Learners return to their cumulative 'Shadow-Angle-Height' model (begun in Lesson 1 and revised in each subsequent lesson). Today's revision task: update the model to show that for special sun angles (30°, 45°, 60°), the height of any object can be expressed as an exact surd multiple of its shadow length. Specifically, learners add a new 'exact values branch' to their model diagram and complete the following table within the model:

| Sun Angle | tan(angle) | Height of mango tree (shadow = 9 m) |
|-----------|------------|--------------------------------------|
| 30° | 1/√3 | 9/√3 = 3√3 m |
| 45° | 1 | 9 m |
| 60° | √3 | 9√3 m |

Learners also annotate their model with one new 'wondering' question to post on the DQB in the next lesson, such as: 'Does sin 53° have an exact value too, or only special angles?' or 'What happens to tan as the angle gets very close to 90°?'
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Open your cumulative model from Lesson 1. You have 10 minutes to add the exact-values branch. The table on the board is your guide — complete it in your model using the exact surd values you derived today.' (Write the table shell on the board with the first row completed as a model.) Set a 10-minute timer. Circulate. After 8 min: 'Two more minutes — make sure you have written at least one new wondering question on a sticky note for our DQB.' At 10 min: 'Pens down. Two volunteers to share their wondering question.' Cold-call 2 learners. Post their sticky notes on the DQB 'Questions We Still Have' column. Close: 'Every time we update this model, we get closer to fully answering our driving question. In Lesson 8, we will look at the relationship between complementary angles more deeply — your wondering questions today will guide us there.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision (NGSS SEP 2 — Developing and Using Models): Revisiting and annotating the same model across all 10 lessons externalises the learners' evolving mental models, making conceptual growth visible. Adding exact values as a new 'branch' shows learners that today's knowledge is an extension of — not a replacement for — prior knowledge. The 'wondering question' routine sustains inquiry momentum into the next lesson.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) The model table shows exact surd expressions (9/√3 rationalised as 3√3, or left as 9/√3 — both accepted if exact). (2) At least one wondering question per learner is written and posted. (3) The model clearly distinguishes between 'approximate' ratios (Lessons 4–6) and 'exact' ratios (Lesson 7). Exit check: teacher collects 6 books (2 high, 2 mid, 2 emerging) to review model accuracy before Lesson 8.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) Did learners complete both geometric constructions correctly within the 18-minute window? If not, which construction caused the most difficulty — the equilateral triangle (30-60-90) or the isosceles right-angled triangle (45-45-90) — and what scaffolding will you provide at the start of Lesson 8? (2) How many passport cards posted on the DQB contained exact surd values rather than decimals? If fewer than 70% of groups achieved this, plan a 5-minute 'exact vs. approximate' mini-lesson at the start of Lesson 8. (3) Did the Kisumu mango-tree anchor successfully motivate the derivation, or did learners ask 'why does it matter if it's exact?' — if so, prepare a stronger real-world precision argument (e.g., engineering tolerances for a Nairobi bridge). (4) Which learners' wondering questions showed the deepest thinking, and how can their questions shape the inquiry direction of Lesson 8 on complementary-angle relationships? (5) Were there learners who already knew the special angle values from prior tutoring — how were they challenged to go deeper (e.g., explaining the geometry to peers, deriving values for 0° and 90° as limits)?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners observed — through their own geometric constructions — that halving an equilateral triangle of side 2 produces a right-angled triangle with sides 1, √3, and 2, and that an isosceles right-angled triangle with legs 1 has a hypotenuse of √2. They observed that the trigonometric ratios read from these triangles are exact expressions involving √2 and √3, not messy decimals, and that these values appeared consistently in the video and reference reading.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Learners learned that five special angles (0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°) have exact trigonometric ratios derivable from geometry alone — no calculator needed. They learned that sin 30° = 1/2, cos 30° = √3/2, tan 30° = 1/√3; sin 45° = cos 45° = 1/√2; sin 60° = √3/2, cos 60° = 1/2, tan 60° = √3; and the boundary values at 0° and 90°. They also learned that sin θ = cos(90°−θ) for complementary angles, and that tan 90° is undefined because the Adjacent side of the triangle collapses to zero.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Learners explained how the exact values connect back to the anchoring phenomenon: if the sun in Kisumu made an angle of exactly 60° with the ground, the height of the mango tree (shadow = 9.0 m) would be exactly 9√3 metres — a precise, unrounded answer. They explained this on their passport cards and in their cumulative models, articulating that exact values allow more reliable calculations in real-world measurement tasks such as surveying, construction, and navigation across Kenya.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: special angles, trigonometric ratios, exact values, 30-60-90 triangle, 45-45-90 triangle, equilateral triangle, isosceles right-angled triangle, sin cos tan, SOH CAH TOA, Pythagoras theorem, surds, complementary angles, passport card, driving question board, shadow height tree, Kisumu, Kenya, Grade 10 mathematics, CBE, KICD, Trigonometry I

LESSONS.title: Special Angles: Solving Real Problems Without a Calculator

slo.purpose: Learners apply exact trigonometric values of special angles (0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°) to solve real-world problems involving heights and lengths, and revise their cumulative right-triangle model to incorporate these values and the identity tan = sin/cos.
slo.knowledge: Learners know and recall the exact trigonometric ratios for special angles (0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°) derived from the 30-60-90 and 45-45-90 right triangles, and recognise the identity tan θ = sin θ / cos θ.
slo.skills: Learners calculate unknown side lengths and angles in right-angled triangles using exact special-angle values without a calculator, and annotate their cumulative model diagrams with these values.
slo.attitudes: Learners appreciate the power of exact mathematical relationships — that a builder in Eldoret, a farmer in Kericho, or an architect in Nairobi can design structures precisely without relying on electronic tools.
slo.keyInquiry: How do we apply trigonometric ratios in day-to-day activities? How are the trigonometric ratios of acute angles related to each other?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lessons 1–7 the class established SOH CAH TOA, explored similar triangles, and used calculators/tables to find ratios for arbitrary angles. Now, in Lesson 8, they discover that for five special angles the ratios are exact — no approximation needed. This unlocks the ability to solve the Eldoret builder's roof problem and to revisit the Kisumu mango-tree phenomenon with precision, advancing the driving question by showing that the angle–ratio relationship is not just consistent but, for special angles, perfectly exact.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous materials. Ensure scissor use for cutting card triangles is supervised. Learners should keep desks clear of clutter when passing card-triangle manipulatives between groups.

LESSONS.overview: Lesson 8 of 10 in the evidence-gathering sequence. Learners begin by predicting exact ratio values for 30°, 45°, and 60° using symmetry and prior knowledge of equilateral and isosceles right triangles. They then derive the exact values through a structured card-triangle activity and validate them against their calculator/table findings from earlier lessons. The Eldoret builder's roof-rafter context anchors the Explain phase: groups solve a 60°-pitched roof of 5 m span using exact values only. A short paired reading on special angles in Kenyan construction and engineering grounds the mathematics in authentic practice. Groups then revise their cumulative annotated right-triangle model to embed the special-angle table and the tan = sin/cos identity. The lesson closes with a DQB update that maps new evidence onto the anchoring phenomenon (Kisumu mango-tree shadow and sunbeam angle).

framework.learnerExperience: Each learner receives a blank 3-column table with headers: Angle | sin | cos | tan. They individually fill in their BEST GUESS for 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90° — using only memory, symmetry reasoning, or any prior knowledge. They annotate a small sketch of a right-angled triangle to show which sides they think grow or shrink as the angle increases from 0° to 90°. They write a one-sentence prediction: 'I think sin 60° is ___ because ___.' Predictions are held (not shared yet) in their exercise books to be revisited after derivation.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the anchoring phenomenon image (student in Kisumu, mango-tree shadow, clinometer reading 53°). Say: 'Last lesson we used calculators and tables. Today I want you to FORGET the calculator. Look at your blank table — what do you already know or can reason about these special angles? You have 4 minutes, working alone. Go.' Set a visible 4-minute timer. Circulate silently; do NOT confirm or correct — only observe. After 4 minutes: 'Keep your tables face-down. We will come back to them.' Cold-call 3 learners: 'Achieng, what did you write for sin 90°?' WAIT 12 seconds. 'Otieno, same question — agree or disagree with Achieng and say why.' WAIT 12 seconds. 'Fatuma, what about tan 45°? Take your time.' WAIT 15 seconds. Record the three predicted values on the board WITHOUT revealing correctness. Say: 'By the end of this lesson you will know with certainty — and you will never need a calculator for these five angles again.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Activation of Prior Knowledge + Prediction Journal. Learners surface informal geometric intuitions (e.g., 'at 45° the triangle looks symmetric so sin = cos') before formal derivation. Writing a prediction sentence forces them to articulate reasoning, creating a cognitive gap that motivates the derivation activity.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans completed prediction tables during circulation. Observable indicator: learner has written a numerical fraction or decimal (not left blank) for at least 3 of the 5 angles AND has provided a written justification sentence. Flag learners who have all cells blank — seat them next to a peer who wrote something for the pair-derivation in Phase 2.

framework.learnerExperience: Groups of 4 receive two pre-cut card triangles: (A) an equilateral triangle (side = 2 cm, labelled) and (B) a right isosceles triangle (legs = 1 cm each). Step-by-step task card instructs them to: (1) Fold Card A along its altitude to produce two 30-60-90 right triangles — measure/label all three sides using Pythagoras (½ base = 1, hypotenuse = 2, altitude = √3). (2) Use Card B (45-45-90) — label hypotenuse = √2. (3) Complete a group special-angles table by computing sin, cos, tan for 30°, 45°, 60° from these side lengths. (4) Use the pattern of the table to deduce sin 0°, cos 0°, sin 90°, cos 90°, and tan 90° (undefined). (5) Check: does tan θ = sin θ ÷ cos θ hold for every special angle in their table? Record 'YES/NO + working' for each angle. Groups then open their calculators ONLY to verify: e.g., does sin 30° on calculator = 0.5? They record: 'Calculator confirms: sin 30° = 0.5 ✓'. Finally, each learner flips over their prediction table from Phase 1 and marks each cell ✓ (correct) or ✗ (incorrect).
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute card triangles and task cards. Say: 'This is a geometry detective task. Your card triangles hold the secret of ALL special angles. Work through the steps in order — do NOT skip to the calculator yet.' Allow 18 minutes for Steps 1–5. Circulate; at the 8-minute mark cold-call one group: 'Kamau, what did your group get for the altitude of the equilateral triangle? How did you get √3?' WAIT 12 seconds. If groups stall on √3, prompt: 'What theorem connects sides of a right triangle? Use it on the 30-60-90 triangle.' At the 14-minute mark say: 'Now check your tan column — does tan = sin ÷ cos? Show the working for 60° right now.' WAIT 10 seconds, then ask one group to read their check aloud. After groups complete Step 5, bring class together: 'Turn over your prediction table. Mark each cell. Hands up — who got sin 45° exactly right in Phase 1?' Count hands aloud. 'Interesting. Now we know the exact values. Write them in your notes in a boxed table — you will use this for the rest of secondary school.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Manipulative-Based Derivation (Concrete → Abstract). Physical folding of the equilateral triangle makes the origin of the 30-60-90 ratios visible and memorable — learners SEE why sin 30° = 1/2 rather than memorising a fact. The predict–verify loop (prediction table → derivation → calculator confirmation → self-marking) creates metacognitive awareness of what was intuited correctly versus what needed formal derivation. Connecting tan θ = sin θ / cos θ algebraically confirms the identity discovered numerically in Lesson 7.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect ONE group special-angles table per group (rotate which group each lesson). Observable indicator: all 15 cells (5 angles × 3 ratios) are correctly filled with exact values (fractions / surds), AND the tan = sin/cos check is shown with working for at least 2 angles. Groups with errors in the √3 row receive targeted correction before Phase 3.

framework.learnerExperience: Part A — Eldoret Builder Problem (Guided): The teacher projects or draws on the board: 'A builder in Eldoret is constructing a roof pitched at 60°. The horizontal span of the roof is 5 m (so the half-span, from wall to ridge, is 2.5 m). Calculate the length of one roof rafter (the sloping side) using exact values only.' Learners identify the right triangle (half-span = adjacent = 2.5 m, angle = 60°, rafter = hypotenuse). They write: cos 60° = adjacent / hypotenuse → ½ = 2.5 / rafter → rafter = 5 m. They also calculate the ridge height using tan 60° = height / 2.5 → √3 = height / 2.5 → height = 2.5√3 m ≈ 4.33 m. Part B — Paired Reading: Each pair reads a short 200-word article (printed on the task card) titled 'Special Angles in Kenyan Construction and Land Survey' which describes how Jua Kali artisans in Gikomba Market, Nairobi, use 45° and 60° angles in metalwork without calculators, and how surveyors in the Rift Valley use 30-60-90 geometry to mark land boundaries. After reading, pairs answer 3 questions: (1) Why are special angles useful to an artisan without a calculator? (2) A Gikomba welder makes a triangular brace at 30° to a 4 m horizontal beam — how long is the brace (hypotenuse)? (3) Reconnect to the phenomenon: if the Kisumu mango-tree clinometer had read exactly 60° instead of 53°, and the shadow was still 9.0 m, what would the tree height be using an exact value?
framework.teacherMoves: For Part A, say: 'No calculators. This is Eldoret — the builder has only a tape measure and this table you just derived. Sketch the right triangle first, label it, THEN choose your ratio.' Allow 6 minutes. Cold-call one learner to draw the triangle on the board: 'Wanjiku, come and label the sides for us.' WAIT 10 seconds. After she labels: 'Class, do you agree with how Wanjiku has identified opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse? Show thumbs up / thumbs down.' Correct any mislabelling before proceeding. Walk through the cos 60° step together, then release groups for tan 60° step independently (3 minutes). For Part B, say: 'Read carefully — look for the connection between what the artisan does and what you just derived.' After 8 minutes of paired reading and questions, cold-call 2 pairs for Part B Q3: 'Omondi, what tree height did you get? Show your working on the board.' WAIT 12 seconds. Confirm: tan 60° = h/9.0 → √3 = h/9.0 → h = 9√3 ≈ 15.6 m. Say: 'Notice — same method as in our Kisumu phenomenon, but now exact. The angle and the shadow length are all we need.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Context Transfer + Worked Example Analysis. Anchoring the exact-values skill in the Eldoret builder scenario and the Gikomba artisan reading makes the mathematics purposeful. The phenomenon reconnect question (Part B Q3) is a deliberate transfer task: learners must carry the SOH CAH TOA structure from Lessons 1–7, substitute a special angle, and produce an exact answer — demonstrating that their understanding is not context-bound. This is the NGSS Science & Engineering Practice of 'Using Mathematics and Computational Thinking' applied to engineering design contexts.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher collects paired answer sheets for Part B Q2 and Q3. Observable indicators: (1) Correct right-triangle sketch with labelled sides for Q2; (2) Working shows cos 30° or sin 60° = ½ used correctly to give hypotenuse = 8 m for Q2 (note: hypotenuse = 4/cos 30° = 4/(√3/2) = 8/√3 = 8√3/3 m, OR if using sin: sin 30°=opp/hyp so hyp = 4/sin 60° — accept both approaches with correct working); (3) Q3 answer of 9√3 m with tan 60° = √3 substituted explicitly. Flag pairs with incorrect triangle orientation for Phase 4 support.

framework.learnerExperience: Each group reviews the class Driving Question Board displayed at the front. The DQB has two columns: 'What we have figured out' and 'What we still wonder about.' Groups receive two sticky notes each — one green (new evidence) and one orange (new wonder/question). On the GREEN note they write one specific claim from today's lesson, citing evidence: e.g., 'We figured out that tan 60° = √3 exactly, so a builder can calculate a rafter length with only arithmetic.' On the ORANGE note they write one unresolved question, e.g., 'What happens when the angle is NOT a special angle and we have no calculator — is there another method?' Groups post their notes on the DQB. The teacher reads 3–4 notes aloud and facilitates a 3-minute whole-class discussion connecting new evidence to the anchoring phenomenon: 'How does knowing exact values for special angles sharpen our answer to the driving question about the Kisumu mango tree?'
framework.teacherMoves: Point to the DQB and say: 'In Lessons 1 through 7 we gathered evidence that a consistent angle–ratio relationship exists and that SOH CAH TOA lets us calculate heights. Today we added something new — exact values. What should go on our board?' Allow 4 minutes for sticky-note writing. As groups post notes, read one green note aloud: 'This group says __. Class, do you agree this is evidence that helps answer the driving question? Why?' WAIT 12 seconds. Cold-call one student who has not yet spoken this lesson: 'Njeri, where would you say we are on answering: how can an angle tell us the height of a tree?' WAIT 15 seconds. Summarise: 'We now have a complete toolkit for special angles. The driving question is nearly answered — but Lesson 9 will test whether this works beyond special angles, at any angle, for any tree in Kenya.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Collaborative Sense-Making via Driving Question Board (Argument from Evidence). The DQB is a public, cumulative record of the class's epistemic progress. Green/orange sticky notes structure the difference between settled evidence and productive uncertainty — a direct parallel to the NGSS practice of 'Engaging in Argument from Evidence.' Requiring a cited evidence statement on the green note prevents vague claims and builds the habit of evidence-backed reasoning.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher photographs the DQB after notes are posted. Observable indicator: each group's green note contains (a) a specific trigonometric claim (names a ratio and angle) AND (b) a real-world application reference. Orange notes that reveal persistent misconceptions (e.g., 'Is sin always smaller than cos?') are flagged for Lesson 9 opening address.

framework.learnerExperience: Each group retrieves their cumulative annotated right-triangle diagram begun in Lesson 1 and progressively revised in Lessons 2–7. Today's revision task (on a printed 'Model Update Card 8') asks them to: (1) Add a special-angles mini-table inside or beside the triangle, showing exact sin, cos, tan values for 30°, 45°, 60° using √ notation. (2) Add a label showing the identity tan θ = sin θ / cos θ with an arrow connecting the three sides of the triangle. (3) Annotate two real contexts directly on the diagram: (a) the Eldoret builder's 60° roof with rafter = 5 m labelled, and (b) the Kisumu mango tree with shadow = 9.0 m and height = 9√3 m labelled. (4) Write one 'Model Claim' sentence at the bottom: 'Our model now shows that for special angles, the exact ratios derived from equilateral and isosceles right triangles allow us to calculate real heights with no calculator.' Groups share their revised model with one adjacent group for a 2-minute peer critique: 'Is the special-angles table correct? Is tan = sin/cos labelled? Are both Kenyan contexts annotated?' Peer group initials the model if satisfied.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute Model Update Card 8. Say: 'Your model has grown across eight lessons. Today it gains its most powerful upgrade — exact values and the tan identity. Take 12 minutes to complete all four update steps.' Circulate; at 6 minutes prompt any group still on Step 1: 'Move to Step 2 — you can refine the table later.' At 10 minutes: 'Begin peer critique now.' During peer critique, stop one group sharing: 'Mwangi, what did your peer group say was missing from your model?' WAIT 10 seconds. After peer critique: 'Raise your hand if your model was initialled — good. If not, you have 2 minutes to add the missing element.' Close by saying: 'Your model is now a working reference tool. In Lesson 9 we will test it against angles that are NOT special — does it still hold? Bring this model to every remaining lesson.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Progressive Model Revision (Cumulative Diagrammatic Modelling). The annotated right-triangle diagram is the class's shared explanatory model — equivalent to a scientific model that is iteratively refined as new evidence is gathered. Adding the special-angles table and tan = sin/cos identity in Lesson 8 makes explicit the algebraic structure underlying the numerical patterns learners observed in earlier lessons. Annotating Kenyan contexts directly on the model reinforces that the model is not abstract but explanatory of real phenomena. This enacts the NGSS practice of 'Developing and Using Models.'
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher collects one model per group at the end of the lesson (rotate groups each lesson). Observable indicators: (1) Special-angles mini-table present with all 9 values correct in surd/fraction form; (2) tan = sin/cos identity labelled with directional arrow on the triangle; (3) Both Kenyan context annotations (Eldoret rafter = 5 m; Kisumu tree = 9√3 m) present; (4) Model Claim sentence is specific and references exact values. Models missing 2 or more elements are returned with a targeted correction prompt for the start of Lesson 9.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, consider the following questions: (1) During the card-triangle derivation, did all groups successfully fold the equilateral triangle and apply Pythagoras to find √3? If not, was the difficulty conceptual (Pythagoras) or procedural (the folding)? Plan a 5-minute Pythagoras review opener for Lesson 9 if needed. (2) In the Explain phase, how many learners correctly identified cos 60° (not sin 60°) as the ratio linking the rafter to the half-span? Mislabelling opposite/adjacent is a persistent error — note names of learners who made this error for targeted questioning in Lesson 9. (3) Did the DQB orange notes reveal any misconceptions about the scope of special angles (e.g., learners thinking ALL angles have exact values)? If so, use this as the Lesson 9 opening hook. (4) Were the peer critique conversations during model building substantive — did peers actually check the √ notation, or did they initial without scrutiny? Consider modelling one peer critique exchange explicitly at the start of the next model-building phase. (5) Overall: Is the class ready to extend to non-special angles using tables and interpolation in Lesson 9, or does the tan = sin/cos identity need another application cycle?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners folded equilateral and isosceles right-triangle card cutouts, measured and labelled all sides using Pythagoras, and read off exact sin, cos, and tan values for 30°, 45°, and 60°. They verified these values against calculator outputs and self-marked their Phase 1 prediction tables.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: For special angles (0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°), the trigonometric ratios are exact values derivable from the geometry of equilateral and isosceles right triangles — no calculator is required. The identity tan θ = sin θ / cos θ holds for every special angle. These exact values can be applied directly to real problems such as calculating roof-rafter lengths for a 60°-pitched roof in Eldoret.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Using exact special-angle values and the relationship tan θ = sin θ / cos θ, learners can calculate unknown side lengths in any right-angled triangle whose acute angle is a special angle — including the height of the Kisumu mango tree if the sunbeam angle were exactly 60°. This advances the driving question by showing that the angle–ratio relationship is not just consistent and calculator-dependent, but for special angles it is perfectly exact and derivable from pure geometry.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: trigonometry, special angles, sin 30 cos 30 tan 30, sin 45 cos 45 tan 45, sin 60 cos 60 tan 60, 30-60-90 triangle, 45-45-90 triangle, exact values, tan equals sin over cos, SOH CAH TOA, right-angled triangle, roof rafter, Eldoret builder, Kisumu mango tree, shadow height, clinometer, Pythagoras theorem, surd notation, square root 3, square root 2, Kenya construction, Grade 10 mathematics, CBE mathematics, Trigonometry I

LESSONS.title: Angles of Elevation and Depression: Reading Heights from the Sky
LESSONS.aresKeywords: angle of elevation, angle of depression, clinometer, horizontal line, line of sight, tangent ratio, right-angled triangle, height calculation

slo.purpose: To enable learners to use angles of elevation and depression together with trigonometric ratios to calculate heights and distances in real Kenyan contexts without direct measurement
slo.knowledge: Learners will define angle of elevation and angle of depression, distinguish them from each other, and state that both are measured from the horizontal line of sight to the line of sight toward the object
slo.skills: Learners will (1) draw and label diagrams showing angles of elevation and depression, (2) use a clinometer to measure an angle of elevation outdoors, (3) apply the tangent ratio to calculate an unknown height or distance from a measured baseline and angle, and (4) compare results across groups and evaluate sources of error
slo.attitudes: Learners demonstrate accuracy and intellectual honesty by recording clinometer readings honestly and discussing why group results differ rather than copying a neighbour
slo.keyInquiry: How do we apply trigonometric ratios in day-to-day activities?
slo.purposeInStoryline: Lesson 9 converts the abstract sine-cosine-tangent relationships built in Lessons 1 to 8 into a direct measurement tool: students physically measure an angle of elevation and use tangent to compute a height, bringing the driving question almost fully into view before the final consolidation in Lesson 10
slo.safetyNotes: During the outdoor activity learners must not point the straw of the clinometer toward the sun. Groups must remain within the school compound boundary. If the compound is unavailable the indoor tall-wall alternative is used.

LESSONS.overview: Learners open by revisiting the anchoring phenomenon: the student in Kisumu used a 53 degree sun angle and a shadow length to find the tree height using tangent. Today they ask: what if there is no shadow — can we still find the height? The lesson introduces the airport glide-path as a supporting phenomenon, establishes the formal definitions of angle of elevation and depression, and culminates in an outdoor clinometer activity at the school flagpole or water tank. Learners measure, calculate, compare group results, and update their cumulative model and the Driving Question Board with the new evidence that any height can be found from an angle and a baseline distance.

framework.learnerExperience: Teacher projects or sketches two images side by side on the board: (A) a passenger looking UP at a Kenya Airways plane approaching Wilson Airport, and (B) the pilot looking DOWN at the runway threshold. Teacher asks: In image A, what angle is the passenger seeing? In image B, what angle is the pilot using to judge the descent? Learners individually write a prediction on a half-sheet: (1) Are these two angles the same or different in size? (2) What would you need to know — besides the angle — to calculate how high the plane is above the ground at a given horizontal distance? After 90 seconds of silent writing, pairs compare predictions for 60 seconds. Two or three pairs share with the class. Teacher records key prediction phrases on the Driving Question Board column labelled Lesson 9 Predictions without affirming or correcting them yet. Teacher then asks learners to sketch a simple side-view diagram of the airport scenario showing the plane, the observer on the ground, and the runway, labelling any angle and length they think are relevant.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher opens with: Recall the mango tree in Kisumu. We knew the shadow length and the sun angle, so we used tangent to get the height. Now imagine the sun is behind a cloud — no shadow. How else could you find the height of something tall? Wait 10 seconds. Take a pair share now. After pairs share, teacher says: Today we meet a new scenario — a plane descending to Wilson Airport in Nairobi. I want you to predict before we study anything. Project or draw the two images. Pose the two written-prediction questions. Circulate silently for 90 seconds — do not affirm or negate. Cold-call three pairs using lolly sticks. Record their exact words on the DQB. Then say: Keep your prediction sheet. By the end of the lesson you will know whether your prediction was right.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Eliciting prior knowledge through sketch-and-predict activates the tangent work from Lesson 4 and 8 and surfaces any confusion between looking up and looking down. Recording predictions publicly on the DQB creates accountability and gives learners a concrete claim to test.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans written predictions as they circulate. Look for: Does the learner reference an angle AND a length (baseline)? Do any learners already distinguish between looking up and looking down? Flag learners who write only the angle or only a length — these need scaffolding in the Observe Phase. Checkpoint question (verbal, to the class): Thumbs up if your diagram has a horizontal line in it. Thumbs sideways if it does not. Use this to identify who already understands that both angles are measured from the horizontal.

framework.learnerExperience: PART A — VIDEO AND READING (12 minutes). Learners watch a short video (approximately 4 minutes) showing a pilot on final approach to Wilson Airport, Nairobi. The video narrates: the standard glide-path angle is 3 degrees below horizontal. When the plane is 2 000 m horizontally from the runway threshold, how high is it? After the video, learners read a one-page illustrated excerpt (provided as a printed handout or displayed on screen) that defines: (i) angle of elevation — the angle measured upward from the horizontal to the line of sight toward a higher object, (ii) angle of depression — the angle measured downward from the horizontal to the line of sight toward a lower object, and (iii) that these two angles are alternate interior angles and therefore equal in size when the observer and the object are at the same base level. The reading includes two labelled diagrams: a person looking up at a water tower in Nakuru (elevation) and a lifeguard at Mombasa beach looking down at a swimmer (depression). PART B — GUIDED WORKED EXAMPLES (8 minutes). Teacher works two examples on the board with full learner participation via cold-call: Example 1 (Elevation) — A student stands 15 m from the base of the school water tank. The angle of elevation to the top of the tank is 62 degrees. Find the height of the tank. Draw, label, write tan 62 degrees equals height divided by 15, solve: height equals 15 times tan 62 degrees equals 15 times 1.8807 equals 28.2 m. Example 2 (Depression) — A ranger at the top of a 40 m watch-tower at Maasai Mara spots a vehicle on the flat plain. The angle of depression is 28 degrees. How far is the vehicle from the base of the tower? Draw, label, write tan 28 degrees equals 40 divided by distance, solve: distance equals 40 divided by tan 28 degrees equals 40 divided by 0.5317 equals 75.2 m. Learners copy diagrams and working into their exercise books. PART C — AIRPORT GLIDE-PATH PROBLEM (5 minutes, pairs). Each pair solves: The Wilson Airport glide-path angle is 3 degrees. When the plane is 2 000 m from the runway threshold horizontally, calculate the height of the plane above the ground. They use tan 3 degrees equals 0.0524 from tables or Lesson 5 calculator skills. Expected answer: height equals 2 000 times 0.0524 equals 104.8 m.
framework.teacherMoves: Before the video, teacher says: As you watch, listen for the glide-path angle and the horizontal distance. Sketch what you see — pause and draw if you need to. After the video, distribute the reading. Say: Read sections one and two only — you have 3 minutes. Then I will ask you to define both terms in your own words without looking at the page. After reading, cold-call 4 students (use lolly sticks) to define angle of elevation and angle of depression. For each definition, teacher says: Does anyone want to add to that? Wait 5 seconds after each addition. For Example 1, teacher says: Before I write anything, who can tell me which trigonometric ratio links the opposite side and the adjacent side? Wait 8 seconds. Cold-call. After the ratio is named, teacher says: Good — we are going to use tangent today because we always know the horizontal distance and want the vertical height — or vice versa. Pose the glide-path pair task and say: You have 5 minutes. Show every step. I will cold-call two pairs to present on the board.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The observe phase moves from real video (Wilson Airport) to formal definition to worked example to immediate application — a gradual release model. Anchoring the definitions in Kenyan contexts (Nakuru water tower, Mombasa beach, Maasai Mara watch-tower, Wilson Airport) keeps meaning local and concrete. The glide-path problem connects directly to the Lesson 9 supporting phenomenon and provides the first numerical evidence that angles of depression solve real navigation problems.
framework.formativeAssessment: After the four cold-calls on definitions, teacher asks the class: Show me on your mini-whiteboard or notebook — draw the angle of depression from a point. Scan for common error: learners who draw the angle from the vertical rather than the horizontal. Correct immediately with a sketch. After the glide-path pair task, teacher cold-calls two pairs to write working on the board. Check: (1) Is the diagram present and labelled? (2) Is tan used correctly? (3) Is the answer reasonable — about 105 m for a plane 2 km out? If a pair uses sin or cos instead of tan, ask: Which sides do you know? Which ratio uses those two sides? Wait 6 seconds.

framework.learnerExperience: OUTDOOR CLINOMETER ACTIVITY (approximately 12 minutes total, including transition). Groups of four receive a pre-assembled clinometer (a protractor taped to a 30 cm cardboard strip, with a straw taped along the straight edge and a plumb-line — a piece of string with a small stone — hanging from the centre hole of the protractor). Each group also receives a 20 m measuring tape and a data-recording sheet. Procedure: (1) One member holds the clinometer and looks through the straw at the top of the flagpole or water tank. (2) A second member reads the angle between the plumb-line and the 90-degree mark on the protractor — this is the angle of elevation. (3) A third member measures the horizontal distance from the observer to the base of the object. (4) All four members record the data independently on their sheets. The group calculates the height: height equals distance times tan(angle of elevation). Groups are told to take two readings from two different distances and calculate the height both times to check consistency. Back in class, each group writes its two height estimates on the board. If the outdoor activity is not feasible due to rain or space, an indoor alternative is used: teacher has pre-measured the height of one wall or a mark near the ceiling. Groups use the clinometer from a measured distance inside the room and compare their calculated height to the actual measured value. After results are on the board, teacher facilitates a whole-class discussion: Why do the groups get slightly different answers? What are the sources of error? Learners suggest: parallax in reading the clinometer, measuring tape not held horizontally, not accounting for the observer eye height above the ground. Teacher notes that the observer eye height (approximately 1.5 m) should be added to the calculated height for a more accurate answer — this is a refinement moment.
framework.teacherMoves: Before going outside, teacher says: You are now going to do exactly what the student in Kisumu did — but instead of a shadow angle, you will measure the angle of elevation directly with a clinometer. The clinometer is your protractor turned into a field instrument. Demonstrate the clinometer technique for 60 seconds indoors: Hold it flat, look along the straw, let the plumb-line hang freely, read the angle. Then say: Two rules: do not point the straw at the sun. Stay within the school boundary. You have 8 minutes outdoors. When you return, every group writes both height estimates on the board. Transition takes 1 to 2 minutes. While outdoors, circulate to each group. Ask: What angle are you reading? Which side is the opposite? Which is the adjacent? Back indoors, facilitating the error discussion, teacher says: Look at the values on the board. They range from X to Y. Should they all be the same? Wait 5 seconds. What could cause the spread? After learners suggest reasons, teacher says: This is what scientists and engineers call measurement uncertainty — it is not failure, it is honesty about the limits of our tools. How might a professional surveyor reduce this error? Wait 8 seconds. Cold-call two students.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The outdoor clinometer activity is the kinesthetic peak of the entire ten-lesson sequence — learners physically enact the very process that the Kisumu student used conceptually in Lesson 1. Comparing group results and discussing error turns the activity into genuine scientific inquiry rather than a recipe exercise. The eye-height refinement introduces the idea of systematic error correction, deepening understanding of the measurement-calculation chain.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks each group data-recording sheet during the outdoor phase. Confirm: angle is recorded in degrees, distance in metres, calculation shows tan ratio set up correctly, units are stated. Back indoors, assess the board values: if one group is an outlier (e.g., all others get 8 to 10 m for a flagpole and one group gets 20 m), invite that group to re-examine their diagram — did they read the complementary angle by mistake? Exit check: each learner writes one sentence stating one source of error in their clinometer reading and how it could be minimised.

framework.learnerExperience: Each group receives two sticky notes of different colours. On the first (green), they write new evidence added to our answer of the driving question: We can find the height of any object — tree, tank, flagpole, or aircraft — using the angle of elevation from a known horizontal distance and the tangent ratio. On the second (orange), they write one remaining question or limitation: e.g., What if the ground is not flat? What if we cannot measure the baseline directly? What if we are on a hill? Groups post their sticky notes on the DQB in the Lesson 9 column. Teacher reads three green notes aloud and asks the class: Compare this new evidence with what we added in Lesson 4 when we first used tangent on the mango tree. What is new today? Expected responses: today we measured the angle ourselves rather than being given it; today we used the angle of elevation rather than the sun angle; today we also met the angle of depression. Teacher then reads two orange notes and says: These are exactly the kinds of questions professional surveyors in Kenya ask. We will begin to address them in Lesson 10.
framework.teacherMoves: After groups post their sticky notes, teacher says: Let us look at how far we have come. Point to the DQB Lesson 1 column. In Lesson 1 you predicted an angle could reveal a tree height. In Lesson 4 you used tangent on the mango tree for the first time. In Lesson 9 you actually went outside, measured an angle yourself, and computed a height that you can verify by measuring the real flagpole. How confident are you now that the driving question is answered? Show me with fingers — 1 is not at all, 5 is completely answered. Scan the room. Most should show 4. Say: One lesson remains because real problems in Kenya — surveying, construction, aviation — involve complications. Your orange sticky notes just identified some of them. Well done.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The two-colour sticky-note protocol separates evidence from remaining questions, teaching learners to distinguish what is known from what is still open — a core scientific literacy practice. Reading selected notes aloud and connecting them to earlier DQB entries makes the cumulative knowledge-building arc visible to every learner.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher photographs the DQB after sticky notes are posted. At a glance, check: Do the green notes contain the key idea (tangent, angle of elevation, baseline)? Do the orange notes show genuine conceptual reach (non-flat ground, inaccessible baselines) rather than trivial questions? If green notes are vague (e.g., we learned about angles), prompt the group: Can you name the specific ratio and the specific measurement you need?

framework.learnerExperience: Learners open their cumulative annotated right-angled triangle diagram that has been built across Lessons 4, 7, and 8. They add two new labelled elements: (1) An eye symbol at one vertex with an arrow going up to the opposite vertex labelled Angle of Elevation and a second arrow going down labelled Angle of Depression, with a note that both are measured from the horizontal. (2) The generalised formula: height equals baseline times tan(angle of elevation) or horizontal distance equals height divided by tan(angle of depression). Below the diagram, each learner writes a two-sentence connection back to the anchoring phenomenon: In the Kisumu mango-tree scenario, the sun angle of 53 degrees acted as an angle of elevation from the tip of the shadow to the top of the tree. Today I used the same relationship — tangent of the elevation angle times the baseline equals the height — with a clinometer instead of a shadow. One learner from each group shares their two sentences with the class. Teacher collects or photographs the updated models for formative records.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher says: Take out your cumulative model. You are going to add today's learning in the same way you added the special-angle values in Lesson 8 — as permanent features of the diagram, not on a separate page. Give learners 4 minutes to annotate. Circulate and look for three things: Is the horizontal line drawn at the observer point? Is elevation labelled as upward from horizontal and depression as downward from horizontal? Does the formula use tan (not sin or cos)? After 4 minutes, cold-call one learner per group to read their two-sentence phenomenon connection aloud. After each reading, teacher asks the class: Does this statement correctly connect the angle to the tangent ratio and to a real height? Thumbs up or sideways. If thumbs are sideways, ask: What needs to change? Wait 5 seconds before accepting suggestions. Close by saying: In Lesson 10 we bring all of this together — sine, cosine, tangent, special angles, elevation, and depression — across three Kenyan contexts. Bring your full cumulative model.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Adding to the same cumulative diagram across multiple lessons externalises the conceptual structure of the unit and forces learners to see each lesson as building on rather than replacing previous knowledge. The two-sentence phenomenon connection is a micro-explanation task that assesses whether the learner can link formal mathematics to the real-world context — the highest level of the intended learning.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect or photograph the updated cumulative models. Evaluate against three criteria: (1) Angle of elevation and depression both labelled correctly relative to the horizontal — not the vertical. (2) Formula height equals baseline times tan(angle) present. (3) Two-sentence connection mentions tangent, an angle, and the mango-tree or clinometer context. Use a simple three-point rubric (3 — all criteria met, 2 — two criteria met, 1 — one or zero criteria met) to identify learners who need additional support before Lesson 10.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, consider: Did the outdoor clinometer activity run within the time allocation? If it overran, which phase was shortened and did that affect the model-building quality? Were learners able to articulate the difference between angle of elevation and angle of depression consistently, or is confusion remaining? Did the error-analysis discussion generate genuine reasoning about measurement uncertainty, or did learners simply list errors without explaining their effect on the calculated height? Review the photographed cumulative models: are the diagrams becoming richer and more connected across lessons, or are learners treating each lesson as isolated notes? Plan for Lesson 10: identify the two or three learners whose models show the weakest phenomenon connections — give them a targeted prompt at the start of Lesson 10 before the integrated problem set begins.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: We observed that when a student looks up at the top of the school flagpole and measures the angle with a clinometer, the horizontal distance to the base and that angle are enough information to calculate the height — without climbing the pole or using a shadow.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: We learned that the angle of elevation is measured upward from the horizontal to the line of sight toward a higher object, the angle of depression is measured downward from the horizontal to the line of sight toward a lower object, and in both cases the tangent ratio connects the angle to the opposite side (height or depth) and the adjacent side (horizontal distance).
summaryTablePrompt.explained: We explained these observations using the tangent ratio: height equals baseline times tan(angle of elevation). This is the same relationship used in Lesson 4 to find the mango-tree height from the sun angle, confirming that the driving question can be answered for any tall object — tree, flagpole, water tank, or aircraft on a glide path — as long as we can measure an angle and a baseline distance.

LESSONS.title: Real-Life Applications, DQB Closure & Final Model Revision

slo.purpose: To consolidate and apply trigonometric ratios of acute angles in integrated Kenyan real-life contexts, close the Driving Question Board, and produce a final annotated model that connects all unit concepts back to the anchoring mango-tree phenomenon.
slo.knowledge: Learners demonstrate that trigonometric ratios (sin, cos, tan and their reciprocals) for acute angles define consistent relationships between angles and side lengths in right-angled triangles using SOH CAH TOA, that special angles (0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°) yield exact, memorable ratios derived from 30-60-90 and 45-45-90 triangles, and that complementary-angle identities (sin θ = cos(90°−θ)) link all six ratios into a unified framework applicable at any scale.
slo.skills: Learners select appropriate trigonometric ratios to solve multi-step problems involving heights, distances, and angles in surveying, telecommunications, and aviation contexts; interpret clinometer and shadow data; present mathematical reasoning to peers; and annotate a cumulative model diagram showing concept-to-phenomenon links.
slo.attitudes: Learners appreciate the power and elegance of trigonometric ratios as practical tools used by Kenyan surveyors, engineers, and pilots; show confidence in presenting solutions; and value collaborative sense-making as a means of resolving uncertainty.
slo.keyInquiry: How do we apply trigonometric ratios in day-to-day activities? How are the trigonometric ratios of acute angles related to each other?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This is the culminating lesson of the unit. Learners bring together every concept — SOH CAH TOA, special angles, reciprocal ratios, complementary relationships, and multi-step problem solving — to tackle three integrated Kenyan problems (a plot boundary in Kisumu, a telecommunications mast in Kericho, and a glide path at Wilson Airport). They then return to the original mango-tree phenomenon to give a complete, evidence-based Final Explanation, close every card on the Driving Question Board, and revise their individual models into a polished, annotated final diagram that shows exactly how trigonometric ratios answer the driving question.
slo.safetyNotes: No physical hazards in this lesson. Ensure learners handle printed problem cards and rulers carefully. If the session moves outdoors for a shadow-measurement extension, remind learners to avoid staring directly at the sun and to stay within the school compound boundary.

LESSONS.overview: Lesson 10 is the unit's Final Explanation lesson. In the Predict phase learners revisit their very first shadow-and-tree predictions from Lesson 1 and articulate what they now believe — and why. In the Observe phase groups rotate through three rich Kenyan application stations (Kisumu plot survey, Kericho telecom mast, Wilson Airport glide path), gathering evidence by solving and cross-checking multi-step trigonometry problems. In the Explain phase each group presents one station solution and the class co-constructs the complete SOH CAH TOA → special angles → reciprocals → complementary-identities chain that answers the driving question. The DQB phase closes every prediction and question card posted across all ten lessons. Finally, in the Model Building phase each learner submits an individually annotated final model diagram — a right-angled triangle labelled with all six ratios, the original mango-tree data, and arrows connecting each ratio to the Kenyan real-life context in which it was most powerfully used.

framework.learnerExperience: Each learner retrieves their Lesson 1 prediction card (kept in their science journal or returned by the teacher) on which they wrote how a shadow could reveal a tree's height. Working individually for 3 minutes, they write a 'Then vs Now' comparison: what they predicted in Lesson 1 and what they now know, using precise mathematical language (ratio, opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse, sin, cos, tan, angle of elevation). They share with a shoulder partner, then three pairs are cold-called to read both halves aloud. The anchoring phenomenon is re-displayed: Achieng's 1.6 m height casts a 1.2 m shadow; the mango tree's shadow is 9.0 m; the sun's angle is approximately 53°. The class is asked: 'Using everything you now know, could you calculate the mango tree's height in more than one way? Predict how many distinct methods you can use — write the number and name each method.' Learners record predictions silently (2 minutes).
framework.teacherMoves: Display the original phenomenon image (shadow diagram from Lesson 1) on the board. Say: 'Before we solve anything new today, I want you to travel back to Lesson 1. Pull out your prediction card.' Distribute cards if collected. Allow 3 minutes of silent individual writing. Then say: 'Turn to your shoulder partner — you have 90 seconds. Partner A shares Lesson 1 prediction; Partner B shares what they now know. Then swap.' After 90 seconds call TIME. Cold-call 3 pairs (choose one high-confidence, one mid-confidence, one who has been quieter across the unit). Use the sentence frame on the board: 'In Lesson 1 I predicted ___. Now I know ___ because ___.' After each pair, ask the class: 'Does anyone want to add or respectfully challenge?' Allow 10 seconds WAIT TIME after each challenge prompt. Then say: 'Now, eyes on the board. The same mango tree, the same shadow, the same angle. How many distinct mathematical methods can you now use to find the tree's height? Write a number and list the method names. You have 2 minutes — go.' Circulate and note the range of numbers students write (expect 2–4 methods: shadow ratio / similar triangles, tan ratio directly, sin with hypotenuse, cos with hypotenuse after finding hypotenuse). After 2 minutes cold-call 4 learners. Record their method counts on the board without evaluating. Say: 'We will return to this question at the end of the lesson to see who was right.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: 'Then vs Now' Reflective Prediction. By forcing learners to articulate the gap between their Lesson 1 intuition and their current understanding in precise mathematical language, this strategy activates the full unit's schema, surfaces any residual misconceptions before the final explanation, and creates cognitive readiness for synthesis. Naming multiple solution methods primes learners to see trigonometric ratios as a connected system rather than isolated procedures.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Each learner's 'Then vs Now' card uses at least two of the following terms correctly — ratio, opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse, sin, cos, tan, angle of elevation, complementary. Teacher scans cards during circulation and flags any learner who cannot write a 'Now' statement with mathematical vocabulary for targeted support during the Observe phase stations.

framework.learnerExperience:
Groups of four rotate through three problem stations (12 minutes each, 1-minute transition). Each station card is printed on coloured paper and contains a diagram, all given measurements, and three scaffolded sub-questions progressing from identify → calculate → verify.

Station A — Kisumu Plot Survey (Green card): A land surveyor in Kisumu needs to find the width of a rectangular plot whose diagonal boundary fence runs at 37° to the longer side. The diagonal measures 48 m. Sub-questions: (i) Identify which trigonometric ratio links the 37° angle, the diagonal (hypotenuse), and the shorter side (opposite). (ii) Calculate the shorter side to 1 d.p. (iii) Verify using Pythagoras' theorem.

Station B — Kericho Telecom Mast (Yellow card): A Safaricom engineer needs to know the height of a new mast in Kericho. She stands 30 m from the base on flat ground and measures the angle of elevation to the top as 60°. Sub-questions: (i) Draw and label the right-angled triangle. (ii) Use the exact value of tan 60° = √3 to calculate the mast height exactly, then to 2 d.p. (iii) If a support wire runs from the top of the mast to her feet, calculate the wire length using the exact value of cos 60°.

Station C — Wilson Airport Glide Path (Blue card): Aircraft approaching Wilson Airport in Nairobi descend on a glide path of 3°. A pilot is at an altitude of 300 m when she begins her final approach. Sub-questions: (i) Use sin 3° (tables or calculator) to find the slant distance the aircraft travels from that altitude to touchdown. (ii) Use cos 3° to find the horizontal ground distance. (iii) The co-pilot says the angle of elevation from the threshold of the runway to the aircraft is 87°. Explain why this makes sense using the complementary angle identity sin θ = cos(90°−θ).

All groups record solutions on a shared Group Answer Sheet. Groups are told they must be ready to present any one station — the teacher will randomly assign at the start of the Explain phase.
framework.teacherMoves: Before releasing groups say: 'Each station is a real Kenyan context. Your job is not just to get a number — it is to show the reasoning chain: identify the ratio, set up the equation, solve, and verify or interpret. All four group members must be able to explain the solution.' Assign groups to starting stations (use pre-assigned colour cards). Set a visible 12-minute timer. At the 11-minute mark say: 'One minute — make sure every member can explain your solution.' Sound a soft signal for transitions. During rotations, circulate and listen for: (a) correct ratio identification, (b) correct substitution, (c) use of exact values at Station B, (d) complementary angle reasoning at Station C. At Station B, probe with: 'Why can we use √3 exactly instead of a decimal? What does that tell us about special angles?' Allow 10 seconds WAIT TIME before accepting responses. At Station C, probe: 'The pilot's angle is 3° and the co-pilot says 87°. Where did 87° come from? Convince me with an identity.' After all three rotations, say: 'Before we move to presentations — quickly, without looking at your sheet — can the mango-tree problem be solved the same way as Station A or Station B? Thumbs up for A, sideways for B, down for both, fingers crossed for both.' Take a quick visual poll and say: 'Hold that thought.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: Rotating Evidence Stations with Kenyan Contexts. Rotating stations ensure every group encounters all three application domains (surveying, telecommunications, aviation), building the evidence base that trigonometric ratios are universally applicable — not just a classroom procedure. The progression from identify → calculate → verify within each station mirrors the scientific practice of 'Planning and Carrying Out Investigations' (NGSS SEP 3) and 'Using Mathematics' (NGSS SEP 5). Requiring all group members to be presentation-ready instills individual accountability within collaborative structures.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Group Answer Sheets show a labelled diagram, a correctly written ratio equation (e.g., tan 60° = h/30), and a numerical answer with units at each station. (2) At Station B, learners write '30√3 m' or '51.96 m' — either is acceptable but exact form is preferred. (3) At Station C, learners explicitly write 'sin 3° = cos 87° because 3° + 87° = 90°' — any group that writes only a numerical answer without the identity explanation is flagged for the Explain phase discussion. Teacher records which groups show the complementary-identity reasoning unprompted.

framework.learnerExperience: Each group presents one assigned station solution (5 minutes per group, 15 minutes total). Presenters write their triangle diagram and calculation on the board; the class listens and completes a 'Gallery Critique' slip — one thing correct, one question or extension. After all three presentations, the teacher facilitates a whole-class synthesis: learners co-construct a 'Trigonometry Concept Map' on the board that shows SOH CAH TOA at the centre, branching to special angles (30°, 45°, 60°), reciprocal ratios (cosec, sec, cot), complementary identities, and arrows pointing to each of the three Kenyan stations. A final arrow points back to the mango-tree diagram on which learners demonstrate all applicable methods. The class then formally answers the driving question in writing: 'The angle a sunbeam makes with the ground allows us to calculate height because…' (minimum three sentences, must include ratio, similar triangles, and angle of elevation).
framework.teacherMoves: Randomly draw station assignments from a hat. Say: 'Group ___, you are presenting Station A — Kisumu. You have 30 seconds to choose your presenter and your diagram-drawer. Go.' While groups prepare, distribute Gallery Critique slips. During each presentation, stand to the side and listen. After each presentation, ask the class: 'On your slip — write one thing they did correctly and one question or extension you have. You have 60 seconds.' Cold-call two learners per presentation to share their slips (target different learners for each presentation — aim for at least 8 different voices across the three presentations). Respond to each slip with: 'Can the presenting group answer that question?' Allow 10 seconds WAIT TIME. After all three presentations, say: 'Now we build the map together. I'll draw the trunk — SOH CAH TOA. Who can give me the first branch? What concept connects directly to SOH CAH TOA?' Wait 10 seconds, then cold-call. Build the concept map collaboratively, asking 'Where does this branch connect?' for each new concept. When the map is complete, point to the mango-tree diagram: 'How many of these branches can we use to find the tree's height? Come and draw an arrow for each one.' Invite 4 volunteers. Finally, say: 'In your journals, write the answer to our driving question. You have 4 minutes. Use ratio, similar triangles, and angle of elevation — all three terms must appear.' After 4 minutes cold-call 3 learners to read aloud. Post exemplary responses on the board.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: Gallery Critique + Collaborative Concept Mapping. Gallery Critique (one correct, one question) trains learners to evaluate mathematical reasoning rather than just absorb it, embodying 'Engaging in Argument from Evidence' (NGSS SEP 7). The co-constructed concept map externalises the unit's conceptual architecture, making the connections between ratio types, special angles, and real-life applications visible and permanent — directly building the sensemaking capacity needed for the final model. Writing the driving-question answer forces every learner to synthesise individually before the model phase.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Each learner's Gallery Critique slip names a specific mathematical step (not 'they drew a triangle' but 'they correctly identified tan = opposite/adjacent'). (2) The written driving-question answer contains all three required terms (ratio, similar triangles, angle of elevation) in a logically connected argument. (3) Volunteers drawing arrows on the mango-tree diagram can justify each arrow verbally — teacher asks 'Why this ratio for this triangle?' and accepts answers that name the given sides.

framework.learnerExperience: The full Driving Question Board — accumulated across all 10 lessons — is displayed. Cards are organised into four colour-coded columns: Predictions (Lesson 1), Questions (Lessons 1–9), Evidence (Lessons 2–9), and Claims (ongoing). Working in pairs, learners receive a printed DQB Summary Sheet listing every card's text. They spend 8 minutes marking each card with one of three stamps: ✓ Fully Answered, ~ Partially Answered, ? Still Uncertain. Pairs then share their stamps with another pair to form a group of four and reach consensus on any disagreements. The teacher leads a whole-class DQB review: each original Lesson 1 prediction card is read aloud, the class votes on its status, and the teacher moves it to a 'Resolved' column. Key unresolved '?' cards (if any) are acknowledged as productive open questions — extensions beyond Grade 10 scope (e.g., 'What happens with angles greater than 90°?' — pointing forward to Trigonometry II). The driving question is read aloud one final time: 'How can the angle a sunbeam makes with the ground allow us to calculate the height of any tree, building, or hill in Kenya without measuring it directly?' The class answers chorally using the sentence frame drafted in the Explain phase.
framework.teacherMoves: Uncover the full DQB (all cards visible). Say: 'This board tells the story of our learning across 10 lessons. Every question you ever asked, every prediction you ever made — it is all here.' Give pairs the DQB Summary Sheet. Say: 'In 8 minutes, stamp every card. Be honest — if something is still unclear to you, mark it with a question mark. That is not failure; that is intellectual honesty.' After 8 minutes, say: 'Find a second pair and compare your stamps. If you disagree, you have 2 minutes to convince each other using evidence from your journals.' After 2 minutes, begin whole-class review. Read each Lesson 1 prediction card aloud. Ask: 'Who predicted this? Raise your hand.' Acknowledge the original predictor by name. Then ask: 'Class — fully answered, partially answered, or still uncertain? Show me fingers: one, two, or three.' Record the majority vote and physically move or annotate the card. For any '?' cards, say: 'This is a beautiful question — it belongs to Trigonometry II, Grade 11. Write it in your journal as a question to carry forward.' End by saying: 'Let us answer our driving question together — everyone, on three: one, two, three—' and allow the choral response. Then say: 'Achieng in Kisumu started with a shadow and a protractor. You have just shown that a simple angle connects her to every tree, mast, and runway in Kenya. That is the power of mathematics.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: DQB Closure with Consensus Stamping. Returning to the full DQB makes the unit's learning trajectory visible and honours the epistemic practice of tracking claims against evidence over time — mirroring how scientists and engineers revise understanding iteratively (NGSS SEP 6: Constructing Explanations). Consensus stamping in pairs then fours builds the collaborative reasoning skill mandated by the Core Competency of Communication and Collaboration. Acknowledging unresolved questions as productive extensions models intellectual honesty and intrinsic motivation for further inquiry.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Every pair's DQB Summary Sheet is fully stamped — no blank cards. (2) Disagreements between pairs are resolved by citing a specific lesson's evidence (e.g., 'In Lesson 4 we found that sin 30° = 0.5 exactly, so this prediction is fully answered'). (3) During choral response, the teacher listens for the presence of the words 'ratio', 'right-angled triangle', and 'angle of elevation' — if absent, probes with 'Can someone add the mathematical language?'

framework.learnerExperience: Each learner works individually to produce their Final Annotated Model — a revision and completion of the model they have updated across all 10 lessons. The model must include: (1) A large right-angled triangle labelled with θ, Opposite, Adjacent, Hypotenuse. (2) All six trigonometric ratios written as fractions beside the triangle. (3) A special-angles table (0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°) for sin, cos, tan. (4) The complementary identity sin θ = cos(90°−θ) written and illustrated with one example. (5) The original mango-tree data (Achieng: height 1.6 m, shadow 1.2 m; tree shadow 9.0 m; angle 53°) worked through using at least two distinct methods, with answers circled. (6) Three labelled arrows — one pointing to the Kisumu plot, one to the Kericho mast, one to the Wilson Airport glide path — each annotated with the ratio used and why. (7) A 'Final Explanation' box at the bottom answering the driving question in 2–3 sentences. Learners spend 15 minutes on their models. They then do a 'Lesson 1 vs Lesson 10' comparison: they hold their original Lesson 1 model sketch next to the final model and write three differences on a sticky note, which they attach to the final model before submission.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute blank A3 paper (or learners use their last journal page). Say: 'This is your final model — the most important piece of mathematics you will produce in this unit. It should be something you could show a friend in Mombasa or a farmer in the Rift Valley and use it to explain how trigonometry works in the real world.' Display the seven required components as a checklist on the board. Say: 'You have 15 minutes. Work in silence — this is individual. I will be circulating and I may ask you to explain any part of your model to me verbally.' Circulate every 2 minutes. When you see a learner struggling with the mango-tree calculation, crouch to their level and ask quietly: 'Which sides do you know? Which ratio connects them?' — do not give the answer. When you see a strong model, ask the learner: 'Can you add one more thing that would make this model useful to someone who has never studied trigonometry?' At the 13-minute mark say: 'Two minutes — make sure your Final Explanation box is complete and your mango-tree answers are circled.' At 15 minutes say: 'Pause. Take out your Lesson 1 model sketch. Hold them side by side. On a sticky note, write exactly three differences — be specific and mathematical. Attach it to your final model.' Collect all final models. Close the lesson by asking: 'One word — how do you feel about trigonometry now compared to Lesson 1? Whisper it to yourself, then someone brave, share it with the class.' Accept 4–5 responses. End with: 'The mango tree in Kisumu is still standing. Now you have the tools to measure it — and every tree, mast, and mountain in Kenya — without leaving the ground.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Strategy: Cumulative Model Revision with L1 vs L10 Comparison. The final annotated model is the unit's capstone sensemaking artefact — it requires learners to represent, connect, and explain the entire conceptual structure in a single diagram, embodying 'Developing and Using Models' (NGSS SEP 2) at its highest level. The L1 vs L10 sticky-note comparison makes cognitive growth concrete and visible, activating metacognitive reflection. Requiring two distinct methods for the mango-tree calculation confirms conceptual flexibility rather than procedural memorisation.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) The final model contains all seven required components — teacher uses the checklist to score each submission (0–7 points) and returns with written feedback within one lesson. (2) The 'Final Explanation' box uses the words 'ratio', 'right-angled triangle', 'angle', and 'height' in a coherent argument — vague answers ('trigonometry helps us') are flagged for verbal follow-up. (3) The sticky note names three specific mathematical differences (e.g., 'In Lesson 1 I did not know what tan meant; now I know tan θ = opposite/adjacent') — non-specific differences ('I know more now') are returned with the prompt 'Give me the mathematics.' (4) The mango-tree calculation shows at least two correctly labelled methods with matching numerical answers (tree height ≈ 12.0 m from shadow ratio; ≈ 12.0 m from tan 53° × 9.0 m).

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After collecting final models, use the 7-component checklist to score each submission before the next lesson and identify the three most common gaps — likely candidates are: (a) incomplete special-angles tables (missing 0° or 90° entries), (b) the complementary identity stated but not illustrated with a numerical example, and (c) 'Final Explanation' boxes that are procedurally correct but do not reference the anchoring phenomenon. Return models with written feedback and a single starred 'growth question' personalised to each learner's gap. Reflect on: Did the DQB closure reveal any questions that the lesson sequence did not adequately address — for example, questions about obtuse or reflex angles? If so, note these as explicit 'bridge questions' to introduce at the start of Trigonometry II. Consider whether the three Kenyan application stations were equally accessible — Station C (Wilson Airport glide path) requires reading comprehension about aviation that may have disadvantaged learners unfamiliar with airports; in a future iteration, offer an alternative third station set at Jomo Kenyatta International Airport with a more scaffolded diagram. Finally, audit gender participation across all 10 lessons: were female learners (like the phenomenon's protagonist Achieng) equally represented in cold-call responses and group presentations? Adjust cold-call lists in future units to ensure equity.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students observed that the same trigonometric ratios that describe Achieng's shadow-and-tree relationship in Kisumu also govern the angle of a plot boundary fence in Kisumu (Station A), the height of a Safaricom telecommunications mast in Kericho calculated exactly using tan 60° = √3 (Station B), and the glide-path distance of an aircraft approaching Wilson Airport where the complementary-angle identity sin 3° = cos 87° was verified numerically (Station C). They also observed, by holding their Lesson 1 and Lesson 10 models side by side, the measurable growth in their own understanding across the unit.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Students learned that trigonometric ratios (sin, cos, tan and their reciprocals cosec, sec, cot) form a unified, interconnected system — not isolated formulas — in which SOH CAH TOA, special-angle exact values, and the complementary identity sin θ = cos(90°−θ) are all expressions of the same underlying geometric relationship between angles and side-length ratios in right-angled triangles. They learned that this relationship is scale-invariant: it works identically whether the triangle is the small one formed by Achieng's shadow or the large one formed by an aircraft's glide path, because similar triangles preserve ratios. They confirmed that the mango tree's height (≈ 12.0 m) can be found by at least two independent trigonometric methods, validating the consistency of the ratio system.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Students explained that the angle a sunbeam makes with the ground (the angle of elevation) creates a right-angled triangle whose sides are related by fixed trigonometric ratios regardless of the triangle's size, because all triangles with the same angle are similar and therefore preserve the ratio of any two corresponding sides. Therefore, once you know the angle and one side length (a shadow, a measured distance, or an altitude), the trigonometric ratio — sin, cos, or tan — acts as a conversion factor that scales any known length into the unknown height or distance. This is why Achieng, a land surveyor in Kisumu, a Safaricom engineer in Kericho, and a pilot approaching Wilson Airport all use the same mathematical tool: the consistent, angle-determined ratios encoded in SOH CAH TOA.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: trigonometric ratios, SOH CAH TOA, sine cosine tangent, special angles, 30-60-90 triangle, 45-45-90 triangle, angle of elevation, complementary angles, right-angled triangle, opposite adjacent hypotenuse, reciprocal ratios, cosecant secant cotangent, shadow height problem, similar triangles, Kisumu plot survey, Kericho telecommunications mast, Wilson Airport glide path, driving question board closure, final annotated model, Kenya Grade 10 mathematics, Trigonometry I

FINAL_EXPLANATION.instructions:
This is your Final Explanation — your opportunity to show everything you have learned across our 10-lesson Trigonometry I sequence. You have investigated the anchoring phenomenon: a student in Kisumu used her own shadow and the shadow of a mango tree, plus a clinometer angle of 53°, to figure out the tree's height without climbing it. Now it is your turn to explain, clearly and completely, how that is possible. 

Read each section prompt carefully. Write in full sentences. Use correct mathematical vocabulary (trigonometric ratio, sine, cosine, tangent, opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse, angle of elevation, similar triangles). Show all working where calculations are required. Your explanation should be detailed enough that a fellow student who missed every lesson could read your work and fully understand how angles unlock unknown heights. Good luck!

KICD Learning Outcomes addressed: Apply trigonometric ratios to solve problems involving right-angled triangles; establish relationships among sine, cosine, and tangent of acute angles; connect mathematical reasoning to real-world contexts.

Competencies: Communication and collaboration · Critical thinking and problem solving · Creativity and imagination.
Core Values: Responsibility · Integrity · Social justice.
PCIs: Environmental awareness (indigenous trees, land use) · Financial literacy (construction, surveying).

sections.title: Section 1 — The Hidden Pattern: Similar Triangles and Consistent Ratios
sections.prompt:
In our opening lesson, a student in Kisumu noticed that her 1.6 m height cast a 1.2 m shadow, while the mango tree's shadow was 9.0 m long — all at the same moment on the same sunny afternoon.

(a) Draw and label two right-angled triangles: one for the student and one for the mango tree. Mark the height (vertical side), shadow length (horizontal side), and the sun's ray (hypotenuse) on each triangle. Explain why both triangles must contain the same angles.

(b) Using the concept of similar triangles, show how you can calculate the height of the mango tree from the shadow measurements alone. Show your working fully.

(c) Explain what this tells us about the RATIO of opposite side to adjacent side in both triangles. Why does this ratio stay the same even though the triangles are very different in size?
sections.exemplar:
(a) [Diagram shows two right-angled triangles. Triangle 1 — student: vertical side = 1.6 m (height), horizontal side = 1.2 m (shadow), hypotenuse = sun's ray. Triangle 2 — tree: vertical side = h (unknown height), horizontal side = 9.0 m (shadow), hypotenuse = sun's ray. Both triangles share the same sun-ray angle at the base because the sun's rays are parallel at the same moment in time, so the angle of elevation of the sun is identical for both.]

(b) Since the triangles are similar (AA similarity — same sun angle, both have a 90° angle), their corresponding sides are proportional:

Height of student / Shadow of student = Height of tree / Shadow of tree
1.6 / 1.2 = h / 9.0
h = (1.6 × 9.0) / 1.2
h = 14.4 / 1.2
h = 12.0 m

The mango tree is 12.0 m tall.

(c) In both triangles, the ratio (opposite ÷ adjacent) = (height ÷ shadow length) equals 1.6/1.2 = 4/3 ≈ 1.333. This ratio is the same because the triangles are similar — they have identical angles. Whenever an angle is fixed, the ratio of the opposite side to the adjacent side is always the same, no matter how big or small the triangle is. This consistent ratio is exactly what the tangent trigonometric ratio captures.

sections.title: Section 2 — Defining the Three Trigonometric Ratios
sections.prompt:
You have discovered that for a fixed angle, the ratio of two sides in a right-angled triangle never changes. Mathematicians have named three of these special ratios: sine, cosine, and tangent.
